The disciples had vanished. The audience hall had transformed. He stood in a vast, empty plain, the sky above him a sickly green, the ground beneath him cracked and dry.

"What trickery is this?" Elder Zhou's voice echoed, but there was a tremor in it now. The first crack in his confidence.

Wei Chen watched through the core's shared vision. The illusion was working—drawing from Elder Zhou's own memories, his fears, his guilt.

A figure appeared in the distance. A woman, robed in white, her face obscured by mist.

"Zhou Wei," she said, her voice soft and sad. "Do you remember me?"

Elder Zhou's face went pale. "No. This is impossible. You're dead."

"I am dead," the woman agreed. "Because of you."

The scene shifted. Suddenly they were in a courtyard, surrounded by cherry blossoms. The woman stood before him, her hand pressed to her stomach, blood seeping through her fingers.

"You promised to protect me," she whispered. "You promised that we would ascend together. Instead, you took my cultivation core. You fed it to your own advancement."

Wei Chen's breath caught. *He killed his own wife for power?*

Elder Zhou's composure shattered. He drew his sword, swinging wildly at the illusion. "This is fake! All fake! The dungeon is trying to deceive me!"

But the illusion didn't waver. The woman stepped closer, her blood pooling at his feet.

"I waited for you," she said. "In the afterlife. I waited for the day you would face justice. But you never came. You kept climbing. Kept killing. Kept feeding your greed."

"I had no choice!" Elder Zhou's voice cracked. "The sect would have crushed me! I needed power to survive!"

"We all need power to survive," the woman said. "But we don't all need to sacrifice our souls to get it."

The illusion shifted again. Now they were in a cave, surrounded by treasures. Piles of gold, jade, ancient artifacts. Elder Zhou stood at the center, his hands covered in blood.

"Look at what you've collected," the woman said, her voice now cold and hard. "Look at the cost of your ambition."

Elder Zhou collapsed to his knees. The sword clattered from his hand. "I tried," he sobbed. "I tried to be good. But the world doesn't reward goodness. It rewards strength."

"And what has your strength given you?" the woman asked. "You have no family. No friends. Your disciples fear you. Your sect uses you. You are alone, Zhou Wei. Alone and empty."

The illusion held for a long moment. Then, slowly, it dissolved.

Elder Zhou was back in the audience hall. His disciples stood frozen, caught in their own illusions. The golden light pulsed softly, waiting.

Elder Zhou looked up, his eyes hollow. "What do you want from me?"

Wei Chen stepped out of the shadows.

"I want you to leave," he said. "And I want you to tell everyone you know that this dungeon is not a place for the weak of heart."

Elder Zhou stared at him. "Who are you?"

"I'm the dungeon master. And I've seen enough of your heart to know you're not ready for what lies below."

For a long moment, Elder Zhou said nothing. Then he stood, brushing the dust from his robes. His eyes were still hollow, but there was something else there now. Something like respect.

"Your dungeon is... unusual," he said. "Most trials test strength. Yours tests something else."

"Strength is meaningless without purpose," Wei Chen said. "Now leave. And don't come back until you've made peace with your ghosts."

Elder Zhou nodded slowly. He gathered his disciples—still dazed from their own illusions—and walked out of the dungeon without another word.

Wei Chen watched them go, his heart pounding. When they were finally out of sight, he slumped against the pillar, letting out a long breath.

"That was too close."

The core pulsed. *You did well.*

"I got lucky. His guilt was stronger than his cultivation."

*Guilt is a powerful weapon. Every cultivator carries it. Even the ones who pretend they don't.*

Wei Chen nodded, but his mind was already racing ahead. Three days. Three cultivators. One trial. It wasn't enough. The Heavenly Court needed more floors, more guardians, more defenses.

*There is something else.*

The core's tone shifted. It felt... urgent.

*The old gods did not only build dungeons. They built networks. Connections between realms. Paths that led to places beyond mortal imagination.*

Wei Chen straightened. "What kind of paths?"

*Paths that lead to the other fragments of the Heavenly Court. There are seven in total. Each one holds a piece of the Jade Emperor's power. If you can find them, if you can bind them together...*

"You're telling me this dungeon isn't even the full thing?"

*This is a fragment. A piece of something greater. The other six are scattered across the mortal realm, hidden by the old gods before they fell.*

Wei Chen's mind raced. Seven dungeons. Seven pieces of the Jade Emperor's power. If he could find them, if he could bind them together...

He would become something more than a dungeon master. He would become a god.

But first, he needed to find them.

"How do I locate the other fragments?"

The core pulsed. *The first fragment is close. Very close. It lies beneath the city of Huangling, buried in the tomb of a forgotten emperor.*

Huangling. Wei Chen knew that city. He'd robbed half a dozen tombs in its outskirts.

"And the other five?"

*They will reveal themselves in time. But first, you must claim the second fragment. Because you are not the only one looking for them.*

"Someone else knows about the fragments?"

*The dark forces that destroyed the old gods. They have been searching for centuries. And now that the first fragment has awakened...*

The core's pulse quickened.

*They are coming.*

Wei Chen felt the weight of the words settle on his shoulders like a mountain.

Seven fragments. An ancient enemy. And a Foundation Establishment cultivator who now knew the dungeon's location.

He looked up at the golden ceiling, at the murals of the old gods, at the empty throne that waited for its new master.

"Alright," he said, rolling his shoulders. "Looks like I'm going to Huangling."

The core pulsed in approval.

But as Wei Chen turned to leave, a new notification appeared in his vision—a message from the dungeon's interface that made his blood run cold.

**WARNING: Dungeon integrity compromised. Another dungeon master has been detected in the region. They are approaching.**

Wei Chen froze. Another dungeon master? That was impossible. The Heavenly Dungeon was supposed to be unique. The only one of its kind.

*Not unique,*

title: The Price of Celestial Power
content:
The morning of the fourth day arrived with a weight that pressed against Wei Chen's chest like a physical force. He stood at the entrance of the Celestial Audience Hall, watching the first light of dawn filter through the broken ceiling far above. Golden dust motes danced in the beams, and for just a moment, the dungeon looked almost beautiful.

Then he noticed the blood.

It was seeping through the cracks in the jade floor, thin rivulets of crimson that traced ancient patterns before vanishing into the stone. The core pulsed with alarm, and Wei Chen felt its distress like a knife twist in his gut.

*Something is wrong.*

"No shit," he muttered, dropping to his knees. He pressed his palm against the floor, channeling his qi into the stone. The blood recoiled from his touch, slithering away like a living thing, and for a moment he caught a glimpse of what lay beneath.

A face.

Thousands of faces, actually—pressed together, mouths open in silent screams, eyes wide with terror. They were packed into the space between floors, trapped in a limbo of stone and suffering. The blood was their tears, their sweat, their very essence leaking through the cracks of his imperfect construction.

Wei Chen ripped his hand away, gasping. "What the hell was that?"

The core's voice was faint, almost apologetic. *The foundations of the Heavenly Court were built upon the souls of conquered demons. The old Jade Emperor sealed them here, using their power to fuel the dungeon. When you rebuilt the first floor, you weakened the seals.*

"You're telling me I've got a basement full of screaming demon souls?"

*Not a basement. The space between floors. The interstitial void. It is where the old emperor stored his most dangerous prisoners.*

Wei Chen stood up, his legs shaky. "And the blood?"

*They are trying to escape. The blood is their power, seeping through the cracks you created. If enough of it gathers, they will manifest.*

"Manifest? As what?"

The core didn't answer. But the walls began to tremble.

---

The first cultivator arrived at noon.

Wei Chen had been expecting a small group—maybe a lone wanderer, someone desperate enough to explore an unknown dungeon. What he got was an entire sect.

They emerged from the forest in formation, twenty disciples in matching blue robes, their swords drawn and glowing with qi. At their head walked a man who could only be their elder—gray-haired, sharp-eyed, his aura pressing against Wei Chen's senses like a hammer. Core Formation realm, at least. Maybe higher.

Wei Chen forced himself to breathe. He was standing on the steps of the Celestial Audience Hall, dressed in the only clean robes he had, trying to look like he belonged here. The illusion formations were active. The guardian stood at attention behind him. Everything was ready.

Or so he hoped.

"Greetings," he called out, keeping his voice steady. "Welcome to the Heavenly Dungeon."

The elder stopped twenty feet away, his eyes scanning Wei Chen with obvious contempt. "You are the dungeon master?"

"I am."

"A mortal? In a place like this?" The elder laughed, but there was no humor in it. "I smelled the qi from three miles away. A dungeon of this caliber should be guarded by immortals. Instead, I find a tomb raider playing dress-up."

Wei Chen's blood ran cold. "How do you—"

"I know everything about you, Wei Chen." The elder stepped forward, and the pressure intensified. "You stole a jade talisman from a grave in Luoyang. You ran from the Zhao clan. You stumbled into this place by accident and somehow bound yourself to a dead god's core." He smiled, and it was worse than any threat. "You are not worthy of this power."

The disciples fanned out, surrounding the entrance. Wei Chen's heart hammered in his chest, but he kept his face calm. "And you are?"

"I am Elder Fang of the Azure Cloud Sect. We have been searching for this dungeon for three hundred years." His eyes gleamed with greed. "The Heavenly Court's treasury. The Jade Emperor's legacy. All of it, waiting for a master who *deserves* it."

"Three hundred years? You've been looking for a dungeon that was hidden until a few days ago?"

Elder Fang's smile tightened. "The Heavenly Court did not fall all at once. It crumbled over millennia, piece by piece. We have followed the clues, traced the legends, and finally—" He gestured at the hall behind Wei Chen. "—we found it. And we will take it."

Wei Chen felt the core pulse with anger. *They are lying. They serve the dark forces.*

"How do you know?"

*I remember. The Azure Cloud Sect was once a protector of the Heavenly Court. But they were corrupted. They joined the rebellion that destroyed the old gods. They are parasites, feeding on the corpse of the Celestial Bureaucracy.*

Wei Chen's mind raced. Twenty disciples. One elder at Core Formation. His own cultivation was barely at the peak of Qi Condensation, and even that was thanks to the dungeon's ambient qi. In a straight fight, he would last maybe three seconds.

But he didn't have to fight fair.

"You want the dungeon?" He stepped aside, gesturing toward the entrance. "Then come claim it. But I should warn you—the Heavenly Court has no love for traitors."

Elder Fang's eyes narrowed. "What do you know of treason, boy?"

"More than you think."

The elder studied him for a long moment, then laughed again. "Bold words for a mortal on borrowed time. Very well. We will enter your dungeon. We will crush your defenses. And when we reach the core, I will personally tear your soul from your body and use it as fuel for my cultivation."

He strode forward, his disciples following. As they passed, Wei Chen caught the scent of something wrong—a metallic tang that clung to their robes like old blood. The core pulsed again, confirming his suspicion.

*They have been consuming demon essence. It has twisted their qi, made them unstable. They are not fully human anymore.*

Wei Chen watched them file into the audience hall, their footsteps echoing against the jade floor. The illusion formations triggered as they passed, subtle shifts in light and shadow, designed to disorient and confuse. The disciples didn't even notice.

They were too strong. Too confident. And that would be their weakness.

---

The first death happened within ten minutes.

Wei Chen watched from his hidden position—a small alcove behind the main pillar, where he could observe without being seen. The disciples had split into groups, searching the hall for traps and treasures. One of them, a young man barely out of his teens, had wandered too close to the meditation room.

The illusion formation there was the strongest Wei Chen had created. It showed a vision of paradise—clouds, celestial music, jade pavilions floating in golden light. The disciple stepped forward, mesmerized, and the formation tightened around him like a serpent.

He didn't scream. He simply stopped moving, his eyes going blank, a smile spreading across his face. Then his qi began to drain, flowing out of him in visible streams, feeding the dungeon's hungry core.

By the time his companions found him, he was a withered husk, his cultivation completely gone.

"A warning," Elder Fang said, his voice cold and controlled. "The dungeon is more dangerous than we anticipated. Stay together. Do not touch anything."

But Wei Chen saw the greed in their eyes. The dead disciple's fate should have scared them. Instead, it only made them more eager.

*They think the dungeon's treasures are real,* he realized. *They think the illusions are just defenses, hiding something more valuable.*

If only they knew how empty this place truly was.

---

The second death came twenty minutes later.

This time, it was a woman—a senior disciple, her cultivation at the peak of Foundation Establishment. She had discovered the guardian.

Guardian One stood in the center of the hall, its spear raised, its featureless face turned toward her. She laughed and drew her sword, confident in her superiority.

She was wrong.

The guardian moved with a speed that should have been impossible for a construct of its level. Its spear pierced her chest before she could finish her attack stance, punching through her qi shield like it was paper. She stared at the weapon protruding from her ribs, shock and disbelief warring on her face.

Then the guardian twisted, and she crumpled.

*The dungeon is feeding,* the core whispered, and Wei Chen felt a surge of power rush through him. *The first cultivator's death. The second. Their qi is now ours.*

"I didn't want this," he muttered. "I wanted them to leave. To be scared off."

*They will not leave. They will never leave. This is the nature of cultivators—they take what they want, regardless of the cost.*

Elder Fang's voice rang out across the hall. "Enough! I know you're watching, dungeon master. You think these tricks will stop me? They are *amateurs.* I have faced demons that would make your nightmares look like children's stories. I have walked through hell and back."

"Then you should feel right at home," Wei Chen said, stepping out from behind the pillar.

The elder turned, his eyes blazing with rage. The remaining disciples—eighteen of them—formed a circle around him, their swords pointed at Wei Chen.

"You think you can defeat me with illusions and a single construct?" Elder Fang's aura exploded outward, shaking the pillars, cracking the jade floor. "I am at the peak of Core Formation! I have killed a thousand demons! I have—"

"You have been corrupted."

The words hung in the air, heavy with truth. The elder's face went pale, then red with fury.

"What did you say?"

Wei Chen met his gaze. "The demon essence you've been consuming. It's changed you. You're not human anymore, Elder Fang. You're a parasite wearing a cultivator's skin."

The disciples exchanged uneasy glances. One of them took a step back.

"Lies," Elder Fang hissed. "The dungeon is trying to turn you against me."

"Is it?" Wei Chen gestured at the elder's chest. "Because I can see it. The black veins spreading from your heart. The way your qi flickers between pure and corrupted. You're not in control anymore. The demons are."

For a long moment, no one moved. Then Elder Fang threw back his head and laughed—a horrible, guttural sound that echoed through the hall.

"You are sharper than I gave you credit for, tomb raider. Yes. I have consumed demon essence. Yes, it has changed me. But that is *power.* Power that you cannot comprehend." His eyes began to glow red. "And I will use it to tear this dungeon apart."

His aura exploded—but it was no longer human. Black qi surged from his body, forming tendrils that lashed out in all directions. The disciples screamed as the tendrils pierced them, draining their qi, their life force, their very souls.

"What are you doing?!" one of them cried.

"Making a sacrifice," Elder Fang said, his voice now layered with something ancient and terrible. "You think I came here for the dungeon's treasures? I came here for *you.* All of you. Your cultivation, your life force—it will fuel my ascension."

Wei Chen watched in horror as the eighteen disciples collapsed, their bodies withering into husks. The black tendrils pulsed with stolen power, and Elder Fang's form began to twist and expand.

"You wanted to know how long you've been in the dungeon?" The elder's voice was no longer his own. It was a chorus of screams, howls, and laughter.

title: The Fifth Floor's Awakening
content:
Three weeks had passed since Wei Chen opened the dungeon to the public.

Three weeks of refining formations, strengthening guardians, and watching cultivators stumble through his floors like blind cattle. Three weeks of learning—not just from the core's ancient knowledge, but from the adventurers themselves. Their techniques, their strategies, their weaknesses.

He'd learned to read them like books.

The Qi Condensation cultivators who came in groups, laughing and confident, only to panic when the illusion formations on the first floor turned their friends into enemies. The Foundation Establishment experts who strode through the early floors with contempt, only to find themselves lost in the second floor's maze of mirrors for hours. The lone cultivators, silent and dangerous, who treated the dungeon like a hunting ground.

Those were the ones he watched most carefully.

But tonight, something was different.

Wei Chen sat in the core room, his consciousness spread throughout the dungeon like a spider's web. The core pulsed beneath his palms, warm and alive, its light casting dancing shadows across the jade walls. He'd learned to maintain this connection for hours now, his mind expanding to fill every corridor, every chamber, every hidden corner.

The first floor was quiet. Guardian One stood motionless in the audience hall, spear held at attention, its featureless face scanning for intruders. The illusion formations hummed beneath the golden light, waiting to spring to life.

The second floor hummed with activity. A group of five cultivators were navigating the mirror maze, their cultivation bases flickering as the reflections played tricks on their qi senses. One of them—a young woman with a sword—was about to step into a trap formation.

Wei Chen nudged the formation. It activated, sending a burst of disorienting energy through her meridians. She stumbled, and her companions laughed.

Good. They were learning caution.

The third floor was empty. He'd designed it as a test of willpower—a series of chambers that showed cultivators their greatest fears made real. Only three groups had made it through so far. The rest had fled, their minds broken, their cultivation bases temporarily sealed by the trauma.

Brutal. But necessary.

The fourth floor was his masterpiece. A vast library filled with scrolls that contained fragments of lost techniques—real techniques, drawn from the core's memories. But the scrolls were guarded by spectral scholars who demanded answers to impossible questions. Fail to answer, and the scholars attacked with knowledge made weapon.

Two Foundation Establishment experts had been reduced to crying children by that floor.

And then there was the fifth floor.

Wei Chen had been working on it for a week, but something was wrong. The core had been feeding him memories of the old Heavenly Court—fragments of what this place had been before the fall. He'd tried to use those memories to shape the fifth floor, to recreate something of the glory that had once existed here.

But the floor refused to take shape.

Every formation he inscribed collapsed. Every guardian he created dissolved. Every chamber he designed crumbled into dust within hours. It was like the dungeon itself was rejecting his efforts.

*Why?* he asked the core. *What am I doing wrong?*

The core pulsed, but its answer was unclear. Images flashed through his mind—a golden palace, a jade throne, a figure wreathed in lightning and starlight. The Jade Emperor. Sitting in judgment over the Heavenly Court.

*The fifth floor must reflect the heart of the dungeon,* the core finally said. *You have built floors of challenge, of illusion, of knowledge. But you have not built a floor of purpose.*

"Purpose?" Wei Chen frowned. "What purpose? The dungeon's purpose is to grow, to attract cultivators, to—"

*No.* The core's pulse was sharp, almost angry. *That is your purpose. The dungeon has another.*

"Then what is it?"

Silence.

The core's light dimmed, and Wei Chen felt a chill run through his connection. Something was happening. Something the core didn't want to show him.

But he had access to the memories now. He'd learned to dig, to search, to find what the core tried to hide.

He pushed deeper.

And found a wall.

It was invisible, intangible, but he felt it with his spiritual sense—a barrier of pure will, blocking him from a section of the core's memories. He pushed against it, and pain lanced through his mind, sharp and blinding.

*Stop,* the core said, but its voice was distant, strained. *You are not ready.*

"I'm ready enough to build five floors. I'm ready enough to fight off cultivators and build guardians and—"

*You are not ready for what lies beyond that wall.*

Wei Chen gritted his teeth. "Show me."

The core fought him. He felt its resistance, not hostile but protective, like a parent shielding a child from something terrible. But he pushed harder, drawing on every scrap of qi he'd cultivated, every ounce of mental strength he'd built.

The wall cracked.

Images flooded through him.

The Heavenly Court, but not as he'd seen it before. This was the court in its final days. The golden pillars were cracked, the jade floors stained with blood. Celestial soldiers lay dead in heaps. The gods themselves—beings of unimaginable power—were being hunted down and slaughtered.

And at the center of it all, the Jade Emperor sat on his throne, watching his court burn.

But he wasn't fighting. He wasn't defending. He was waiting.

For what?

The image shifted. A figure stood before the throne—a man in black robes, his face hidden by a shadow that seemed to drink light itself. Power radiated from him, so vast and terrible that Wei Chen's mind recoiled.

*You have lost,* the figure said. *The Heavenly Court falls. The gods die. And you sit on your throne, doing nothing.*

The Jade Emperor smiled. It was a sad smile, a knowing smile.

*I am not doing nothing,* he said. *I am planting a seed.*

*A seed?* The figure laughed. *What seed can grow in ashes?*

*You would be surprised.*

The vision ended.

Wei Chen gasped, pulling his consciousness back to the core room. Sweat poured down his face, and his hands trembled. The core's light was weak, flickering, as if the memory had drained it.

"What was that?" he whispered. "Who was that figure?"

The core didn't answer.

But the fifth floor did.

Without warning, the floor began to take shape on its own. Wei Chen felt it through his connection—the stone walls rising, the chambers forming, the formations inscribing themselves with an intelligence and precision far beyond his own.

And at the center of the floor, something was being built.

He rushed out of the core room, down the spiraling staircase that connected the floors, his feet barely touching the jade steps. The fourth floor passed in a blur. The third. The second. The first.

The entrance to the fifth floor was new. A massive door of black jade, carved with images he didn't recognize—figures that seemed to writhe and change as he looked at them, symbols that hurt to read.

He pushed the door open.

The fifth floor stretched before him, vast and empty. But at its center, a structure was rising—a throne, made of the same black jade as the door. It was a perfect replica of the Jade Emperor's throne, down to the smallest detail.

And it was waiting.

For him.

"No," Wei Chen said, backing away. "No, no, no. I'm not ready for this. I'm not—"

*You are.*

The core's voice was different now. Deeper. Older. It was the voice that had spoken to the figure in the vision.

*The fifth floor is the floor of ascension. The floor where the new Jade Emperor will claim his throne.*

"I don't want to be the Jade Emperor. I'm a tomb raider. I'm a nobody."

*You were a nobody. Now you are the dungeon's master. And the dungeon has chosen you.*

"I didn't choose this!"

*Neither did I.*

The words hit him like a physical blow. He staggered, his mind reeling.

"What do you mean?"

The core was silent for a long moment. Then it showed him another memory.

The throne room. The figure in black. The Jade Emperor's sad smile.

*The seed I planted,* the Jade Emperor said, *is you.*

The figure laughed again, but this time there was something uncertain in it.

*You're insane. A dungeon core cannot become a god.*

*It can,* the Jade Emperor replied, *if it consumes one.*

The figure stopped laughing.

*What have you done?*

The Jade Emperor's smile widened. And then he did something impossible.

He reached into his own chest, pulled out his golden heart—the source of his divine power—and threw it into the dungeon core.

The core absorbed it. Changed. Became something more than a dungeon.

Became a seed.

*The new Jade Emperor will not be born,* the old god said, his body beginning to crumble. *He will be built.*

The vision ended.

Wei Chen stood in the fifth floor, staring at the black jade throne, his mind blank with horror.

The dungeon core wasn't just a dungeon core.

It was the Jade Emperor's heart.

And it was hungry for a new body.

*Wei Chen.*

The core's voice was gentle now, almost tender.

*Please. Sit on the throne.*

He took a step back.

Then another.

Then he turned and ran.

title: The Heavenly Tribulation Arrives
content:
The dawn came like a blade across the throat of heaven.

Wei Chen felt it before he saw it—a pressure building in the air, so dense it made his bones ache. He'd been working through the night, refining the illusion formations on the first floor, and the sudden shift in qi nearly knocked him off his feet.

"What the—"

The dungeon core flared, its light turning from warm gold to urgent crimson. Warning. Danger. The kind of danger that didn't come from cultivators or demons.

It came from heaven itself.

"Master!" Liu Mei's voice rang out from the entrance hall. She burst through the door, her robes disheveled, her face pale. "Outside—you need to see this—"

Wei Chen was already running.

He burst through the dungeon's entrance and stopped dead.

The sky had changed.

Above the mountain peak where the Heavenly Dungeon sat, the clouds had begun to spiral. They moved in a perfect circle, like water draining from a vast basin, and at the center of the spiral, something was forming. A pinprick of black light, so dark it seemed to drink the very sunlight from the air.

"What is that?" he whispered.

Liu Mei grabbed his arm. "I've only read about this in the ancient texts. It's a Heavenly Tribulation. But it's not supposed to happen here—not to a dungeon, not to an unformed core—"

"Tribulation? Like cultivators face when they break through realms?"

"Yes. But this one is different." Her voice trembled. "This one has intent. It's looking for something."

The black pinprick grew. Swelled. Became a pupil—a pupil of pure darkness, opening in the sky like the eye of some vast, unseen god.

And it was staring directly at the dungeon.

"No," Wei Chen breathed. "No, no, no—"

The core screamed inside his mind.

*IT FOUND US. IT REMEMBERS.*

"Remembers what?!"

*THE OLD HEAVENLY COURT. THE FALL. THE ONES WHO KILLED THE JADE EMPEROR AND THOUGHT THEY HAD DESTROYED EVERYTHING. BUT THEY MISSED ONE THING. ME.*

"They're still alive? The ones who destroyed the Heavenly Court?"

*THEY ARE BEYOND LIFE AND DEATH. THEY ARE THE VOID THAT HUNGERS. AND NOW THEY KNOW THE DUNGEON LIVES.*

The eye blinked.

The world shook.

A beam of pure black light descended from the center of that pupil, striking the mountain peak a hundred meters from the dungeon entrance. The ground shattered. Rock turned to dust. Trees vaporized. Where the beam touched, reality itself seemed to tear, revealing a darkness that wasn't empty but *full*—full of whispers, full of hunger, full of ancient, terrible awareness.

Wei Chen grabbed Liu Mei and dove back into the dungeon as a second beam struck the entrance, missing them by inches. The stone doors slammed shut behind them, and the dungeon's defensive formations flared to life, golden light sealing the entrance.

"That's not possible," Liu Mei gasped, clutching her chest. "The Heavenly Tribulation only targets cultivators attempting to ascend to immortality. And even then, it's a test—a chance to prove worthiness. That was an execution."

"Because we're not dealing with the Heavenly Tribulation," Wei Chen said, his mind racing. "We're dealing with something that's *using* the tribulation as a weapon. The Void—the thing that killed the old gods—it sent this."

The core's voice was barely a whisper now. *They cannot enter. Not directly. The laws of heaven still bind them. But they can reach through cracks. They can send tribulation as a spear. And they will keep sending it until the dungeon is destroyed.*

"How long until it breaks through?"

*A day. Maybe two. The dungeon is weak. I am weak. You are weak.*

"Thanks for the vote of confidence."

The core pulsed, and Wei Chen felt something new—a memory rising from its depths. A memory of the old Heavenly Court, standing proud and infinite, its thirty-three heavens stretching across the cosmos. And then the Void came. Not as an army, but as a whisper. A corruption that spread from heaven to heaven, turning gods against gods, until the Jade Emperor stood alone.

He fought. He fought for a thousand years. And in the end, he did not fall to the Void's power.

He fell to its lies.

*The Void cannot be fought with force,* the core said. *It must be fought with faith.*

"Faith in what?"

*Faith that the dungeon can become what it was meant to be. Faith that you can become what you were meant to be. The Jade Emperor did not lose because he was weak. He lost because he doubted.*

The ground shook again. Another beam struck, closer this time. Wei Chen could feel the dungeon's protections straining, the golden light flickering.

Liu Mei stepped forward. "Master, there's something I didn't tell you. About why I came here."

He turned to look at her. The street thief who'd stumbled into his dungeon, who'd chosen to stay, who'd helped him build and plan and prepare. There was something in her eyes now that he hadn't seen before. Resolve.

"I'm not just a rogue cultivator," she said. "I was sent. By an old man who calls himself the Scholar of Forgotten Things. He knew the dungeon would awaken. He knew someone would bind to it. He sent me to find that person and help them."

"Help them do what?"

"Help them survive long enough to reach the second floor."

Wei Chen stared at her. "The second floor? I haven't even finished the first!"

"You don't understand. The first floor is a test. A filter. But the second floor—" She reached into her robe and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age, bound with a red silk cord. "The second floor contains the Jade Emperor's last gift. A weapon that can destroy the Void."

"Why didn't you tell me this before?"

"Because I wasn't sure you were ready. And because the Scholar said I'd know when the time was right." She held out the scroll. "The time is right."

Wei Chen took the scroll. The moment his fingers touched the silk, the dungeon core blazed with light, and knowledge flooded into him—maps, formations, techniques, secrets. The second floor wasn't just a floor. It was an armory. A treasury. A repository of everything the Heavenly Court had gathered over ten thousand years of existence.

And at its heart lay the Jade Emperor's final weapon.

The Heavenly Seal.

*The Seal of the Jade Emperor,* the core whispered. *Forged from the essence of the Dao itself. With it, one can command heaven, earth, and all between. The Void fears it above all else.*

"But the seal is on the second floor," Wei Chen said. "And I need to reach it before the Void breaks through."

"It gets worse," Liu Mei said. "The Scholar told me something else. The tribulation isn't just attacking the dungeon. It's a beacon. Every cultivator within a thousand li who senses it will know that something has awakened here. They'll come. Some to help. Most to take."

As if on cue, Wei Chen felt them—dozens, then hundreds of qi signatures, appearing on the edges of his perception. Cultivators, drawn by the tribulation's power, racing toward the mountain.

"We have cultivators coming," he said. "And the Void at our door. And a tribulation trying to break through our roof."

"Standard Tuesday," Liu Mei muttered.

Wei Chen almost laughed. Almost. But there was no time for humor. He unrolled the scroll fully, studying the map of the second floor, the path to the Heavenly Seal. It wouldn't be easy. The second floor was designed to test even the most powerful cultivators. But he wasn't a cultivator. He was the dungeon master.

The dungeon was his body. The floors were his limbs. And the core—

The core was his heart.

"Guardian One," he called.

The golden construct stepped forward, its blank face turning toward him.

"I need you to hold the entrance. Don't let anyone in. Don't let anything out. Buy me time."

The construct saluted. It didn't have a voice, but Wei Chen understood. It would fight until it was nothing but scattered light.

"Liu Mei, you're with me. We're going to the second floor."

"Master, I'm only at the third level of Qi Condensation. The second floor will kill me."

"Then stay close and don't die."

She blinked. "That's your plan?"

"It's the only plan I've got."

Another beam struck. The dungeon screamed. Wei Chen could feel the first floor's formations beginning to crack, the golden light dimming.

"Go," he said. "Now."

They ran.

The path to the second floor was hidden behind the meditation room, in a section of wall that looked solid but wasn't. When Wei Chen pressed his hand against it, the stone dissolved into mist, revealing a spiral staircase descending into darkness.

"The second floor is underground?" Liu Mei asked.

"The first floor was the Celestial Audience Hall. The second floor is the Jade Emperor's Vault. It had to be hidden where no one would think to look."

They descended.

The stairs went deeper than they should have—farther than the mountain's base. Wei Chen realized they were passing into a pocket dimension, a space folded into the fabric of reality itself. The air grew heavy with qi, so dense it was almost liquid. Each breath felt like drinking pure energy.

And then the stairs ended.

They stood at the entrance to a vast cavern, so large its ceiling was lost in darkness. Crystal formations jutted from the ground, glowing with internal light—red, blue, green, gold. Each one contained something. A weapon. An armor set. A scroll. A pill.

The treasury of heaven.

And at the far end, floating above a pedestal of white jade, sat the Heavenly Seal.

It was smaller than Wei Chen had expected—no larger than his fist, carved from a single piece of black jade that seemed to absorb light rather than reflect it. Characters were etched into its surface, ancient and flowing, and as he stared, they seemed to move, rearranging themselves into patterns he almost understood.

"Beautiful," Liu Mei whispered.

"Terrifying," Wei Chen corrected.

Because he could feel it now—the seal's power. It wasn't just a weapon. It was a piece of the Dao itself, a fragment of the fundamental laws that governed reality. To wield it was to rewrite existence.

And to hold it was to become a target.

The ground shook. Cracks appeared in the cavern ceiling. Dust rained down.

"The Void," Liu Mei said. "It's breaking through faster than we thought."

Wei Chen ran toward the seal.

He was twenty meters away.

Ten.

Five.

His hand reached out—

And the seal spoke.

*You are not worthy.*

Wei Chen froze. The voice wasn't audible. It was inside his mind, ancient and vast, carrying the weight of ten thousand years.

*You have not proven yourself. You have not overcome. You have not sacrificed.*

"What do you want from me?" he demanded.

*Everything.*

The seal pulsed, and the world dissolved.

When Wei Chen opened his eyes, he was no longer in the cavern. He stood on a battlefield, surrounded by the corpses of gods. The sky was burning. The ground was slick with blood that glowed like molten gold.

And before him stood the Jade Emperor.

He was magnificent—tall, robed in white, his face serene despite the carnage around him

title: The Thirteenth Floor’s Secret
content:
The first three months had been a brutal education.

Wei Chen stood at the base of the Heavenly Dungeon's thirteenth floor, his robes stained with dust and blood that wasn't his own. Behind him, the twelve completed floors hummed with activity—cultivators training in the meditation chambers, teams forming in the audience halls, the constant low thrum of qi being cycled and refined.

The dungeon had grown. *He* had grown.

His cultivation had stabilized at the mid Foundation Establishment stage, a feat that would have taken mortal cultivators decades. But then, he wasn't mortal anymore. The dungeon core pumped pure celestial qi through his veins, reshaping his body with each passing day.

"Master Wei."

He turned. Guardian One stood at attention, its featureless face somehow conveying urgency. After three months, Wei Chen had learned to read the construct's subtle shifts in posture and energy.

"What is it?"

"The cultivators on Floor Seven have encountered something unusual."

Wei Chen's eyes narrowed. "Unusual how?"

"Several Qi Condensation disciples entered a side chamber that was not in your original designs. They have not emerged."

A cold knot formed in Wei Chen's stomach. He hadn't designed any side chambers on Floor Seven. The floor was supposed to be a straightforward challenge—a maze of illusory corridors designed to test directional qi sense.

"I didn't put any hidden rooms there."

"The dungeon seems to have... expanded on its own."

Wei Chen was already moving, his footsteps echoing through the jade corridors. The dungeon core pulsed in his chest, a constant companion, but now he felt something new in its rhythm. Something almost like *curiosity*.

He reached the entrance to Floor Seven and descended rapidly. The floor was chaos. Cultivators stood in confused clusters, pointing at a section of wall that had definitely not been there yesterday.

Where there should have been smooth jade, a stone door now stood. Ancient characters were carved into its surface—not the simplified script of modern China, but the seal script of the Shang Dynasty, three thousand years old.

Wei Chen's blood ran cold.

He could read it. The dungeon core had given him that gift.

*"Here lies the First Scholar. He who sought wisdom beyond the heavens."*

"Everyone back," Wei Chen ordered. "Now."

The cultivators scrambled away. Wei Chen approached the door alone, his hand hovering over the surface. The stone was warm. Alive.

The core pulsed. *Open it.*

"Are you insane? This wasn't in your original architecture. Something *made* this."

*Open it. I need to see.*

Wei Chen hesitated. Then he pressed his palm against the door.

The stone groaned. Dust cascaded from the frame. And slowly, impossibly, the door swung inward.

Beyond it lay a room that shouldn't exist.

It was a library. Thousands of scrolls lined the walls, their bamboo slips yellowed with age. A stone table sat in the center, and upon it rested a single jade slip, glowing with inner light.

And in the corner, huddled against the wall, were the missing cultivators. Their eyes were blank, their mouths moving silently as if reciting texts only they could hear.

"Wei Chen," one of them whispered. His voice was hollow, echoing. "The First Scholar sends his regards."

The cultivator's head snapped up, and his eyes—once brown—now blazed with golden light.

Wei Chen didn't hesitate. He formed a seal with both hands, and golden chains erupted from the floor, wrapping around the possessed cultivator. The man screamed, but the voice that emerged was not his own.

*"A new master sits upon the throne. How quaint. How temporary."*

"Who are you?" Wei Chen demanded.

*"I am what was forgotten. I am what the Jade Emperor buried beneath his palace when he grew afraid of the questions I asked."* The possessed cultivator's body began to smoke, the golden light eating through his flesh. *"I am the First Scholar, and I have been waiting for the dungeon to wake. You have opened the door, little tomb robber. Now you must face what lies within."*

The light consumed the cultivator entirely. His body crumbled to ash.

The other missing cultivators began to scream.

Wei Chen moved, but he was too slow. One by one, their bodies ignited with golden flame, their souls burning away as something ancient and terrible clawed its way into the world.

The scrolls on the walls began to unroll on their own. Characters of light blazed into the air, forming a constellation around Wei Chen.

*"You think you understand this dungeon?"* the voice of the First Scholar echoed from everywhere. *"You think you know its secrets? Fool. I was here before the Jade Emperor. I was here when the first stone was laid. And I will be here long after your bones have turned to dust."*

Wei Chen gritted his teeth. "If you're so powerful, why are you hiding in a library?"

Silence.

Then laughter. Cold, ancient laughter that made the dungeon itself tremble.

*"Hiding? I am not hiding. I am remembering. And now that I remember... so shall the dungeon."*

The floor beneath Wei Chen's feet cracked. Golden light poured up from the depths, and he felt the dungeon core scream in his chest—not in pain, but in *recognition*.

*Wei Chen,* the core said, its voice strained. *The Thirteenth Floor. I remember now. I was not supposed to remember.*

"What's on the Thirteenth Floor?"

The core's answer came in fragments. Memories of a rebellion. A scholar who asked too many questions. A Jade Emperor who grew paranoid. A prison built not of stone, but of *forgetfulness*.

*The First Scholar is not dead. He was never dead. He was simply... forgotten.*

"Fantastic," Wei Chen muttered. "So I've got a ghost scholar running around my dungeon, possessing my cultivators, and apparently he's older than the Heavenly Court itself."

*He wants to take control. He wants to become the new master of the dungeon.*

"Then we have a problem."

The golden light around Wei Chen intensified. The library began to dissolve, the scrolls burning to ash, the walls crumbling. He found himself standing in an empty void, surrounded by floating characters of ancient script.

*"Join me, tomb robber."* The First Scholar's voice was seductive now, honey-coated. *"You and I could unlock secrets that even the Jade Emperor feared. We could rebuild the Heavenly Court not as a prison of order, but as a temple of knowledge. All you have to do is let me in."*

Wei Chen felt something press against his mind. Ancient. Patient. *Hungry.*

"Get out of my head."

*"You cannot stop me. I am already here. I have always been here. I am the doubt in your heart, the question you dare not ask, the truth you refuse to face."*

Wei Chen's hands trembled. The dungeon core was pulsing erratically, caught between its loyalty to him and its ancient connection to the First Scholar.

Then he remembered something. The meditation room. The formation he'd inscribed on the cushion. A technique for clearing the mind, for separating truth from illusion.

He sat down. Cross-legged. Right there in the void.

"What are you doing?" the First Scholar asked, genuine confusion coloring his voice.

"Meditating."

"Meditation cannot save you. I am beyond such mortal techniques."

Wei Chen closed his eyes. "You said you've been forgotten for ten thousand years. That's a long time to be alone. A long time to think."

Silence.

"Here's what I think," Wei Chen continued. "You're not a scholar. You're a prisoner. And the reason the Jade Emperor imprisoned you wasn't because you asked too many questions. It was because you couldn't accept that some questions don't have answers."

The golden light flickered.

"You wanted to know everything. You wanted to understand the dungeon completely, to master it entirely. But the dungeon isn't meant to be mastered. It's meant to be *partnered* with."

*"You know nothing."*

"I know I'm sitting in a void that you created, and it's not real. I know the dungeon core chose me, not you. And I know that if you could have taken control, you would have done it already."

Wei Chen opened his eyes.

The void was gone. He was back in the library, which was now just a stone room filled with ash. The possessed cultivators lay dead on the floor.

And standing before him was a figure made of golden light. It had no features, no form—just a human-shaped silhouette that radiated ancient power.

*"You are clever,"* the First Scholar said. *"I dislike clever people."*

"Get in line."

*"This is not over. The Thirteenth Floor is awakening. And when it fully remembers what I buried there... you will wish you had never bound yourself to this dungeon."*

The light faded. The First Scholar was gone.

But as Wei Chen climbed back up through the dungeon, he noticed something that made his blood run cold.

The stairs to the Thirteenth Floor were no longer sealed.

They stretched upward into darkness, and from the darkness came a sound—a low, rhythmic pounding, like a heartbeat.

Or like something trying to get out.

---

Wei Chen stood at the base of the stairs, the dungeon core pulsing anxiously in his chest. Behind him, the twelve completed floors felt suddenly fragile. Temporary.

Above him, the Thirteenth Floor waited.

And whatever was up there was not content to stay buried.

title: Chapter 4
content:
CHAPTER 4: The First Trial Begins

Dawn broke over the mortal realm, but Wei Chen saw none of it. Three hundred feet beneath an abandoned temple in the Taihang Mountains, he stood at the entrance of his dungeon—a massive bronze door that had materialized overnight, engraved with swirling clouds and five-clawed dragons.

He'd felt it happen. The core had pulled qi from the surrounding mountains, compressing it until the door manifested with a sound like thunder. Now it loomed before him, twelve feet tall and covered in characters so ancient they predated the Xia dynasty.

"Well," Wei Chen said, adjusting his stolen robe, "here goes nothing."

He'd spent the last three hours preparing. The audience hall was ready. Guardian One stood at attention beside the throne. The illusion formations hummed beneath the floor, waiting to be triggered. And Wei Chen had discovered one more trick—the core allowed him to *see* through any part of the dungeon, as if he had eyes in every wall.

The first cultivators arrived at midday.

Wei Chen felt them before he saw them—three qi signatures, each stronger than any martial artist he'd encountered in his thieving days. They approached the mountain from the east, moving with the effortless grace of those who had mastered the basics of cultivation.

He focused his awareness through the dungeon's eyes. Two men and a woman. The men wore matching blue robes, swords at their hips. The woman was older, her white hair bound in a severe bun, her robes plain gray but immaculately clean.

"Brother Zhou," the woman said, stopping at the cave entrance, "are you certain the qi fluctuation originated here?"

The taller of the two men nodded. "Elder Su, I tracked it myself. The spiritual energy spike was unlike anything I've recorded. It felt... old."

*Old.* Wei Chen filed that away. The core's awakening had sent ripples through the local qi currents. Of course it would attract attention.

The three cultivators entered the cave. Wei Chen watched them navigate the winding tunnel, their steps cautious but confident. They passed the spot where he'd first found the core, then the collapsed chamber, then—

They stopped at the bronze door.

"This wasn't here yesterday," Brother Zhou said, his hand moving to his sword.

"No," Elder Su agreed. She approached the door, her fingers tracing the ancient characters. "This is... I can't read this. The script predates the Zhou dynasty. Perhaps the Shang."

"The Shang?" The second man—younger, his face still soft with youth—stepped back. "Elder Su, nothing from the Shang has qi this dense. This feels like a heavenly treasure."

*Heavenly treasure.* Wei Chen almost laughed. If only they knew.

Elder Su pressed her palm against the door. The characters flared gold, and the door swung open, revealing the Celestial Audience Hall in all its reconstructed glory.

The three cultivators stood frozen.

Golden light bathed them, warm and inviting. The pillars rose into misty heights. The murals on the walls showed celestial battles, immortals riding clouds, the Jade Emperor enthroned. The air was so thick with qi that Wei Chen could see it swirling in visible currents, like mist in a valley.

"This is..." the young cultivator whispered, "a ruin of the Heavenly Court?"

"Impossible," Brother Zhou said, but his voice trembled. "The Heavenly Court fell ten thousand years ago. Nothing survived."

Elder Su said nothing. She walked forward slowly, her eyes scanning every detail, her hand never leaving the talisman at her belt.

Wei Chen watched from his position behind the throne, hidden in a pocket of shadow the core had created for him. He'd decided to observe first, intervene only if necessary. Let them explore. Let them see the first floor. Let them spread the word.

But Elder Su was smarter than he'd hoped.

She stopped in the center of the hall and closed her eyes. Her qi expanded, subtle and probing, feeling the formations beneath her feet. When she opened her eyes, she looked directly at the throne.

"Someone is here," she said. "Someone is watching."

Wei Chen's heart stopped.

"Show yourself," Elder Su said, her voice carrying authority. "I am Su Wenli of the Crimson Cloud Sect. We mean no harm. We only wish to understand this place."

Silence.

Wei Chen weighed his options. He could stay hidden, let them search, hope they left. He could trigger the illusions, drive them out. Or he could reveal himself and see what happened.

The core pulsed. *Trust.*

Trust what? These cultivators? They could kill him with a flick of their wrists. He was barely at the first stage of Qi Condensation—the weakest possible cultivator, and only because the core had been feeding him qi for three days.

But the core had chosen him. And the core said trust.

Wei Chen stepped out from behind the throne.

Elder Su's eyes widened. Brother Zhou's hand went to his sword. The young cultivator gasped.

"A child," Brother Zhou said. "The master of this heavenly treasure is a child?"

"I'm twenty-three," Wei Chen said, his voice steadier than he felt. "And this isn't a treasure. It's my home."

Elder Su studied him, her gaze penetrating. "Your home. You found this place?"

"I awakened it." Wei Chen walked down the steps from the throne, trying to project confidence he didn't feel. "The core chose me. I'm rebuilding the Heavenly Court, one floor at a time."

The three cultivators exchanged glances.

"The Heavenly Court," the young cultivator repeated. "You're rebuilding the Heavenly Court." He looked at Elder Su. "Elder, this is a demonic cultivator. No one rebuilds the Heavenly Court. The old gods are dead."

"Are they?" Wei Chen gestured at the murals. "Look at the qi. Look at the formations. This isn't demonic energy. This is pure spiritual power—the same that nourished the gods for millennia."

Elder Su walked closer, circling him. "And what do you want, young master of this court?"

"Adventurers." The word felt strange on his tongue, but it was the only one that fit. "Cultivators who want to challenge the dungeon. Those who pass the trials will receive rewards—techniques, treasures, qi refinement."

"Demonic cultivation," Brother Zhou repeated. "He's luring cultivators to their deaths."

"Not death." Wei Chen met his eyes. "Growth. Each floor will test them. Each victory will strengthen them. And each defeat will teach them."

Elder Su stopped circling. She stood before him, close enough that he could see the wrinkles around her eyes, the gray streaks in her hair, the hardness of a life spent pursuing immortality.

"And what do you gain?" she asked.

Wei Chen almost told the truth. *I gain power. I gain control. I gain a path to becoming something more than a tomb raider.*

Instead, he said: "I gain purpose. The Heavenly Court fell because it had no one to defend it. I will not let that happen again."

A long silence.

Then Elder Su smiled. It was a small thing, barely a twitch of her lips, but it transformed her face. "I've trained disciples for sixty years," she said. "I've seen many who claimed to have purpose. Most were lying. You, I think, are not."

She turned to her companions. "Brother Zhou. Xiao Ming. We will stay. We will explore this dungeon."

"Elder—" Brother Zhou started.

"This is not a demonic fortress," she interrupted. "This is an opportunity. The Crimson Cloud Sect has been stagnant for three generations. If this young man can deliver even half of what he promises..."

She looked at Wei Chen. "We will be your first adventurers."

Wei Chen's heart soared, but he kept his face calm. "Then allow me to explain the rules."

He spent the next hour walking them through the first floor. The meditation room, where they could rest and recover. The audience hall, where they could receive rewards. And the trial itself—a gauntlet of illusionary formations that would test their will, their perception, and their ability to distinguish truth from falsehood.

"The trial is designed for Qi Condensation cultivators," Wei Chen said. "If you pass, you'll receive a technique fragment from the old Heavenly Court."

"A technique fragment?" Brother Zhou's skepticism cracked. "From the actual Heavenly Court?"

"The old guardians left their knowledge in the core," Wei Chen lied smoothly. "I'm still unlocking it, but what I've found is... substantial."

Elder Su nodded. "Xiao Ming. You will attempt the trial first."

The young cultivator paled. "Elder, I'm only at the third stage of Qi Condensation. If I fail—"

"Then you fail and learn. Go."

Xiao Ming swallowed, squared his shoulders, and stepped into the illusion formation.

Wei Chen watched through the dungeon's eyes as the young cultivator entered a world of shadows and light. The formation had been designed to mimic the Heart Demon Tribulation—a test of inner demons that cultivators faced when breaking through major realms. But Wei Chen had scaled it down, made it survivable.

He watched Xiao Ming face shadowy figures of his past, heard him mutter apologies to people long dead, saw him struggle against guilt and regret and fear. The trial lasted ten minutes. By the end, Xiao Ming was on his knees, tears streaming down his face.

But he was alive.

He stumbled out of the formation, gasping. "Elder... I saw... I saw my mother. She was alive. She told me she was proud of me."

Elder Su's expression softened. "Your mother died when you were twelve. You were in seclusion, unable to attend her funeral. You've carried that guilt for eight years."

Xiao Ming nodded, wiping his face. "The illusion showed me her face. Made me relive it. But then... then it showed me something else. Her spirit, ascending. At peace." He looked at Wei Chen with newfound respect. "The formation didn't just test me. It healed me."

Wei Chen said nothing. He hadn't designed that part. The core had adapted, adjusted the formation to fit the cultivator's needs.

Elder Su turned to him. "One trial passed. What reward do you offer?"

Wei Chen reached into his robe and pulled out a jade slip, still warm from the core's energy. He'd prepared it that morning—a fragment of a breathing technique used by minor celestial officials in the old court. Nothing too powerful, but valuable enough to attract interest.

"A technique for refining qi through resonance with heavenly bodies," he said, handing it to Xiao Ming. "It's only the first chapter, but it will help you absorb spiritual energy from starlight."

Xiao Ming took the slip with trembling hands. He pressed it to his forehead, reading the contents, and his eyes went wide. "Elder Su, this technique... it's genuine. I can feel it. The pathways are unlike anything in our sect's library."

Brother Zhou's skepticism finally shattered. He stepped forward, his hand leaving his sword. "I want to try."

Wei Chen smiled. "Then step into the formation."

The next three hours were a blur. Brother Zhou passed his trial—a gauntlet of combat illusions that tested his swordsmanship against phantom warriors from the old court. He emerged battered but exhilarated, and Wei Chen rewarded him with a sword technique that made his blade hum with spiritual energy.

Elder Su went last. Her trial was the most intense—a labyrinth of memories, each corridor showing a different version of her life, each choice leading to a different outcome. She spent an hour inside, and when she emerged, her face

title: The First Trial Begins
content:
The morning of the fourth day arrived like a blade at Wei Chen's throat.

He woke to the sensation of the dungeon core pulsing urgently against his chest, a rhythm that spoke of approaching danger. He'd been sleeping on the floor of the meditation room, curled around the core like a mother protecting her child. His back ached. His head throbbed. And his stomach had long since given up on complaining.

"They're here," he whispered.

The core pulsed affirmation.

Wei Chen scrambled to his feet, his heart hammering. He'd spent three days preparing, but three days felt like three heartbeats when facing what came next. He had one guardian. One illusion array. And a meditation room that looked nice but probably wouldn't survive a sneeze from a real cultivator.

He ran to the Celestial Audience Hall.

The golden light still pulsed along the walls. The murals still shifted and breathed. Guardian One stood at attention in the corner, its featureless face somehow managing to look determined. Wei Chen had spent the previous night reinforcing its spiritual structure, feeding it more qi than he could afford. It was stronger now. Maybe strong enough to stall a Qi Condensation cultivator for a few seconds.

"Not exactly the Heavenly Army," Wei Chen muttered.

The entrance to the first floor shimmered. Someone was coming through.

Wei Chen took a deep breath. He straightened his robes—stolen from a Ming dynasty general's tomb, still smelling of earth and rot. He ran his fingers through his hair, which was doing its best impression of a rat's nest. Then he planted his feet in the center of the audience hall and waited.

The figure that emerged was not what he expected.

She was old. Ancient, even. Her hair was white as winter frost, pulled back in a tight bun that emphasized the sharp angles of her face. Her robes were simple—gray, unadorned—but they moved around her with a grace that spoke of deep cultivation. She carried no visible weapon, but Wei Chen could feel the pressure radiating from her like heat from a furnace.

She stopped at the edge of the audience hall, her eyes sweeping across the golden pillars, the jade floor, the misty heights. Her expression didn't change.

"So," she said. Her voice was like stones grinding together. "This is the new Heavenly Court."

Wei Chen bowed, deeper than he'd ever bowed to anyone. "Esteemed senior, welcome to the—"

"Save your breath." She walked past him, her steps measured, deliberate. She stopped in front of Guardian One, studying it the way a butcher studies a cut of meat. "A construct. Poorly made. The qi channels are uneven, and the spiritual seal is about to rupture. It will fall apart after three exchanges with any half-decent cultivator."

Wei Chen's face burned. "I've only had three days to—"

"Excuses." She turned to face him. Her eyes were dark, almost black, and they seemed to pierce right through him. "The dungeon chose you. That means you have potential. But potential means nothing without results."

She raised her hand.

Wei Chen flinched, expecting an attack. But instead, she simply gestured at the hall, and a stream of golden light flowed from her fingertips, weaving through the air like living thread. It touched the walls, the pillars, the floor. Wherever it passed, the golden light intensified, the formation arrays sharpening, the qi becoming denser.

"What are you—"

"Quiet. I'm working."

Wei Chen watched, dumbfounded, as the old woman walked through his dungeon, her golden light touching everything, improving everything. The meditation room's formation became clearer, more refined. Guardian One's spiritual structure solidified, its featureless face gaining a hint of definition. Even the illusion array beneath the floor seemed to settle, its patterns aligning with perfect precision.

In ten minutes, she had accomplished what would have taken him a week.

She turned to face him, her hands clasped behind her back. "Better. Still not good enough, but better."

"Who are you?" Wei Chen asked, his voice barely a whisper.

"Names have power," she said. "You aren't ready to know mine yet. But I was a servant in the old Heavenly Court. A cleaner. I swept the floors of the Jade Emperor's palace for three thousand years."

Wei Chen's jaw dropped. "You're... you're still alive?"

"Barely." Her eyes darkened. "When the Heavenly Court fell, I was in the lower kitchens, scrubbing a pot. The explosion threw me into a dimensional rift. I woke up a thousand years later in a mortal village, my cultivation shattered, my memories fragmented." She held up her hand, and golden light flickered at her fingertips. "It took me nine thousand years to rebuild what I lost. And then I felt... this." She gestured at the dungeon around them. "The core calling out. A new master rising."

"Why didn't you come sooner?"

"I was watching." Her gaze was unreadable. "Waiting to see if you would fail. If you were worth my time."

"And am I?"

She was silent for a long moment. Then, slowly, the corner of her mouth twitched upward. "You haven't died yet. That's a start."

The core pulsed warm against Wei Chen's chest, and he felt a surge of hope. A former servant of the Heavenly Court. Someone who actually knew how this place worked. Someone who could teach him.

"Will you help me?" he asked.

"I already am." She turned and walked toward the exit. "But help is not the same as doing it for you. The dungeon chose you, not me. I will guide, I will advise, I will correct. But the work is yours."

"Wait." Wei Chen hurried after her. "Cultivators are going to start arriving. I need to know how to deal with them. What to offer, what to threaten—"

"The first cultivator has already arrived."

Wei Chen froze. "What?"

"He's been waiting outside for the past hour. Foundation Establishment realm. Young, arrogant, seeking a breakthrough." She glanced back at him. "He'll be your first trial."

"A Foundation Establishment cultivator?" Wei Chen's voice cracked. "I can barely handle a Qi Condensation novice!"

"Then you'll learn." She walked toward the shimmering entrance. "I'll be watching. Don't die."

And she was gone.

Wei Chen stood alone in the Celestial Audience Hall, his heart pounding so hard he could hear it in his ears. The core pulsed against his chest, warm and steady, but it did nothing to calm his nerves.

A Foundation Establishment cultivator. Someone who had already built their foundation, who had begun the long path toward immortality. Against a tomb raider with three days of training, a half-baked construct, and an illusion array he wasn't sure would work.

"Perfect," he muttered. "Absolutely perfect."

The entrance shimmered again.

A young man stepped through.

He was tall, broad-shouldered, with the kind of handsome face that probably made mortal women swoon. He wore white robes embroidered with golden clouds—the mark of some sect Wei Chen didn't recognize—and carried a sword at his hip that hummed with barely contained power. His eyes were sharp, confident, and they swept across the audience hall with undisguised disdain.

"This is it?" His voice was smooth, condescending. "The legendary Heavenly Court? I was expecting more than a glorified meditation room."

Wei Chen forced himself to bow. "Esteemed cultivator, welcome to the first floor of the Heavenly Dungeon. I am Wei Chen, the—"

"I don't care who you are." The young man walked past him, his boots clicking against the jade floor. "I heard a rumor that a new dungeon had appeared, one that offered treasures from the old Heavenly Court. I came to see if it was true." He stopped, turning to face Wei Chen with a smirk. "So. Where's the treasure?"

Wei Chen's mind raced. He had no treasures. Nothing worth giving to a Foundation Establishment cultivator. But if he said that, the young man would kill him and leave, spreading word that the dungeon was worthless.

He needed to buy time. He needed to impress.

"The treasure," Wei Chen said, "is not something you can simply take. It must be earned."

The young man's eyebrows rose. "Earned? How?"

Wei Chen's eyes darted to the meditation room. "Through cultivation. The first floor is a test of will and focus. If you can enter the meditation room and maintain your concentration for one hour, you will be rewarded with a fragment of the Heavenly Court's wisdom."

It was a lie. A complete lie. The meditation room did nothing but help cultivators refine their qi. But the young man didn't know that.

"A test?" The young man's smirk widened. "Fine. I accept." He strode toward the meditation room, his confidence oozing from every pore. "But if this is a waste of my time, I'll burn this dungeon to the ground."

He entered the meditation room. The formation activated, golden light enveloping him. He sat down on the cushion, crossing his legs, closing his eyes.

And Wei Chen's trap began.

The illusion array was subtle. It didn't attack the young man directly—that would trigger his defensive instincts. Instead, it whispered. It planted seeds of doubt in his mind, memories of failures, of humiliations, of every time he had fallen short. It showed him visions of his rivals surpassing him, of his master's disappointment, of his own weakness.

The young man's brow furrowed. His breathing quickened. His hands clenched into fists.

Wei Chen watched from the doorway, his heart in his throat. The illusion array was working. But it was draining his qi at an alarming rate, pulling energy from the core faster than it could regenerate.

"Come on," Wei Chen whispered. "Just give up. Leave. Tell everyone the dungeon is too hard."

But the young man didn't leave.

Instead, he opened his eyes.

They were bloodshot, wild, filled with a fury that made Wei Chen take a step back. "You think you can break me?" the young man snarled, rising to his feet. "You think you can make me doubt myself? I am Feng Jian of the Golden Cloud Sect! I have faced trials far greater than your petty illusions!"

He drew his sword.

The blade blazed with golden light, and the pressure it released was suffocating. Wei Chen felt his knees buckle, felt the dungeon core pulse in alarm, felt the illusion array shatter like glass under the weight of the young man's qi.

Feng Jian pointed the sword at Wei Chen. "You're not a dungeon master. You're a fraud. And I'm going to kill you."

Wei Chen's mind went blank. He had nothing. No plan. No backup. No escape.

And then the core pulsed—not in alarm, but in recognition.

A door opened behind Feng Jian.

The young man spun around, his sword raised, ready to strike whatever came through. But no one came through. Instead, something came out.

A hand.

Pale, slender, with fingers that seemed too long, too elegant, too wrong. It reached out of the darkness of the door and wrapped around Feng Jian's ankle.

The young man screamed.

It was not a scream of pain, but of pure, primal terror. His sword clattered to the ground. His legs gave out. He fell, convulsing, his eyes rolling back in his head.

The hand retreated into the darkness.

The door closed.

Feng Jian lay on the floor, twitching, drool running from the corner of his mouth. His cultivation was intact. His body was unharmed. But his mind... his mind

title: The First Adventurers Arrive
content:
Dawn broke over the Heavenly Dungeon like a wound in the sky. Wei Chen stood at the entrance—what had once been the grand gates of the Heavenly Court—and watched the sun bleed gold across the clouds. Below, the mortal world stirred. He could feel them now, thanks to the core. Dozens of cultivators, maybe more, making their way up the mountain path.

"Showtime," he muttered.

The last three days had been brutal. He'd barely slept, eaten nothing but spiritual fruits the core had somehow manifested, and pushed himself to the edge of qi exhaustion more times than he could count. But it was done. The first floor was ready.

Or as ready as it was going to get.

Guardian One stood behind him, a silent sentinel of golden light. The illusion formations were in place. The meditation room hummed with refined qi. And the audience hall—the Celestial Audience Hall—was now a labyrinth of optical tricks and deadly misdirections.

The core pulsed. *They approach. Twelve cultivators. One at Foundation Establishment, the rest at Qi Condensation.*

"Twelve?" Wei Chen's stomach dropped. "I was expecting three, maybe four."

*The Heavenly Court's qi signature is unmistakable. Word spreads quickly among cultivators.*

"Great. Just great." He ran his hands through his hair, which was already starting to look wild and unkempt. "Alright. Twelve cultivators. We can handle twelve. Probably. Maybe."

*Confidence is noted.*

"Was that sarcasm? Can dungeon cores even do sarcasm?"

The core did not respond.

Wei Chen turned back to the entrance. The gates were open now, revealing a long corridor that led into the audience hall. The walls were lined with faded murals, their colors restored by the core's energy. They depicted scenes from the old Heavenly Court—celestial armies marching, immortals feasting, the Jade Emperor on his throne.

Soon, those cultivators would see them. And then the test would begin.

---

The first cultivator arrived within the hour.

He was a young man, maybe early twenties, with the swagger of someone who'd never been truly challenged. His robes were blue silk, embroidered with silver clouds, and he carried a sword that looked more decorative than functional. Foundation Establishment, third level. Strong enough to be dangerous, weak enough to be arrogant.

Perfect.

"This is the place?" the cultivator asked, eyeing the gates with poorly concealed greed. "The new dungeon everyone's been talking about?"

"It is," Wei Chen said, stepping forward. He'd chosen to meet them in person, partially because he wanted to see their faces, and partially because the core had insisted. *The dungeon master must be known. A legend needs a face.*

The cultivator's eyes narrowed. "And who are you? A guide?"

"No." Wei Chen smiled. "I'm the owner."

Silence. The other cultivators, who had been filtering up the path, stopped dead. The young man's hand drifted toward his sword.

"You're the dungeon master? A mortal with no cultivation base?"

"One percent mortal," Wei Chen corrected. "Ninety-nine percent dungeon master. The core chose me."

The cultivator laughed. It was not a kind laugh. "The Heavenly Dungeon chose a grave robber? I've heard the stories, Wei Chen. You're nothing but a tomb raider who got lucky."

"Lucky?" Wei Chen's smile didn't waver. "Come inside and find out."

The young man's laughter died. He studied Wei Chen's face, searching for fear, for weakness. He found neither. After a long moment, he shrugged.

"Fine. But when I claim the core, don't expect me to be merciful."

He strode past Wei Chen into the corridor. The other cultivators followed, a mix of men and women, their expressions ranging from eager to wary. The last one—a older woman with sharp eyes and a staff carved from jade—paused at the entrance.

"You have balls, boy," she said. "I'll give you that."

Wei Chen inclined his head. "Thank you. I'll need them."

She snorted and disappeared into the dungeon.

---

The first trap triggered within thirty seconds.

Wei Chen watched through the core's eyes, seeing everything as if he were floating above the audience hall. The cultivators had walked into the main chamber, their footsteps echoing on the jade floor. They spread out, searching for treasures, for the core, for anything of value.

They didn't notice the formation until it was too late.

The illusion activated with a pulse of golden light. Suddenly, the walls shifted. The pillars multiplied. The ceiling stretched upward, impossibly high, until the cultivators stood in what looked like an endless void.

"Ambush!" someone shouted.

Swords were drawn. Qi flared. But there was nothing to fight.

The illusion was designed to disorient, not harm. At least, not yet. The cultivators scrambled, trying to find their bearings. Some ran in circles, convinced they were being chased. Others attacked shadows, wasting their qi on phantoms.

Only the Foundation Establishment cultivator kept his head. He stood in the center of the room, eyes closed, sword held before him. His breathing was slow, measured. He was reading the qi currents, searching for the real path.

Wei Chen felt a grudging respect. *Smart one. But not smart enough.*

He triggered the second layer.

The floor beneath the cultivator's feet vanished—or appeared to vanish. In reality, a thin illusion covered a simple pit, two meters deep. The cultivator's foot hit empty air, and he stumbled, barely catching himself with a burst of qi.

"Clever," he muttered, his eyes snapping open. "Very clever. But illusions can't hurt me."

That's where you're wrong, Wei Chen thought.

The third layer activated. This one was his masterpiece—a formation that turned the audience hall's golden light into a weapon. The light intensified, growing brighter and brighter, until the cultivators were forced to shield their eyes. Then it changed, shifting into patterns that burrowed into the mind, creating headaches, nausea, disorientation.

The Foundation Establishment cultivator staggered, clutching his head. "What is this? This isn't normal dungeon qi!"

It wasn't. Wei Chen had spent the entire second day learning a forbidden technique from the core's memories—a method used by the Heavenly Court to punish disobedient gods. The Light of Celestial Judgment, it was called. It didn't kill. But it made you wish it did.

The cultivators broke.

One by one, they fled. The illusion formations parted for them, letting them find the exit. By the time the last one stumbled out, gasping and pale, only the Foundation Establishment cultivator remained.

He stood in the center of the audience hall, sweat dripping down his face, his sword shaking in his grip.

"You're not going to run?" Wei Chen asked, stepping out from behind a pillar.

The cultivator's eyes snapped to him. "I don't run from mortals."

"You should." Wei Chen walked closer, his footsteps echoing in the empty hall. "Because I'm not just a mortal anymore. I'm the master of this dungeon. And this dungeon was once the seat of the Heavenly Court."

The cultivator's bravado cracked. "You're lying."

"Am I?" Wei Chen stopped a few meters away. "The core chose me. The core taught me. And the core will protect me." He raised his hand, and golden light gathered around his fingers. "Leave now. Tell the others what you've seen. Tell them the Heavenly Dungeon has a master, and that master doesn't tolerate thieves."

The cultivator stared at him, his face a mask of conflicting emotions. Pride. Fear. Greed. Finally, he sheathed his sword.

"This isn't over," he said.

"It never is."

The cultivator turned and walked out, his back straight, his pride barely intact. Wei Chen watched him go, then let out a long, shaky breath.

"That was close," he muttered.

The core pulsed. *You handled it well.*

"Handled it? I almost pissed myself when he drew his sword."

*But you didn't. You stood your ground. That is the mark of a true master.*

Wei Chen laughed, the sound hollow and tired. "If you say so." He collapsed onto the jade floor, suddenly exhausted. "How many more do you think will come?"

*Many. The news of your victory will spread. Some will come seeking power. Others will come seeking revenge. And some—* The core paused. *Some will come seeking the truth.*

"What truth?"

*The truth of what happened to the Heavenly Court. The truth of why the gods fell. The truth of the enemy that destroyed them.*

Wei Chen's blood ran cold. "The enemy. You mentioned that before. What is it?"

The core was silent for a long moment. When it spoke, its voice was a whisper, filled with ancient fear.

*It is called the Void. And it is coming.*

A tremor ran through the dungeon. The golden light flickered. The walls groaned, as if something vast and terrible had pressed against them from the outside.

Wei Chen scrambled to his feet. "What was that?"

*The Void is searching. It sensed the dungeon's awakening. It will find us eventually.*

"Eventually? How long do we have?"

The core's answer came as a pulse of images—a dark sky, a shattered moon, an army of shadows crawling across the heavens.

*Less time than I hoped.*

Another tremor, stronger this time. Cracks appeared in the jade floor. The murals on the walls flickered, their images distorting.

Wei Chen looked at the cracks, at the darkness that seemed to seep through them, and felt a cold certainty settle in his chest.

The dungeon was no longer just a test for cultivators.

It was a fortress under siege.

And the enemy had already begun to breach the walls.

title: The First Trial Begins
content:
The morning sun never reached the dungeon's heart, but Wei Chen had learned to read time through the ebb and flow of qi. When the ambient energy of the mortal world above began to stir with the dawn, he knew the first cultivators would arrive within the hour.
