Wei Chen remembered the memory the core had shown him—the armies of darkness, the sky splitting open, the screams of dying gods. The general who had failed to stop them.

"He's been down here for ten thousand years?"

*Waiting. Brooding. Dreaming of redemption.*

Above, the sound of the Foundation Establishment cultivators grew closer. They had found the hidden passage. They were coming.

Wei Chen looked at the sleeping general, then back at the staircase.

He had two choices. Run back up and face the cultivators. Or go deeper and wake the monster.

"I really need a better job," he muttered, and stepped into the forest.

The trees closed behind him, and the general's eyes opened.

title: The Heavenly Tribulation Begins
content:
The first sign of trouble came as a tremor beneath Wei Chen's feet.

He was in the meditation room, refining the third floor's formation arrays when the entire dungeon shuddered. Dust rained from the ceiling. The golden light along the walls flickered, dimmed, then flared back to life.

"The hell was that?" Wei Chen scrambled to his feet, heart hammering.

The core pulsed with something he'd never felt before. Fear.

*Heavenly Tribulation. It has found us.*

"Tribulation? What tribulation? I thought that was a cultivator thing—something that happens when they break through to a new realm."

*This is different. The old gods sealed the Heavenly Court against the chaos beyond. But the seal has been broken for ten thousand years. The chaos has been waiting. Watching. And now it knows someone has taken the throne.*

Another tremor, stronger this time. The walls groaned. Wei Chen could hear stone cracking somewhere above him.

"How does it know? I've been careful. I haven't left the dungeon in weeks."

*You have been feeding the core. Growing the dungeon. Each expansion sends ripples through the spiritual world. To those who know what to look for, you might as well be ringing a bell.*

Wei Chen's mind raced. "Can we hide? Camouflage the dungeon somehow?"

*Too late. It is already here.*

The ceiling above the meditation room split open.

Wei Chen dove sideways as a bolt of black lightning slammed into the spot where he'd been standing. The jade floor shattered. The formation he'd spent hours inscribing was obliterated in an instant.

"What the—that's not normal tribulation lightning! That's—"

*Heavenly Punishment. Forbidden to mortals. Forbidden to gods who have fallen from grace.*

"I'm not a god! I'm a tomb raider who got in way over his head!"

The core had no response to that.

Another bolt struck, this one hitting the wall. The golden light flickered violently. Wei Chen could feel the dungeon's spiritual defenses weakening, the formations he'd so carefully built crumbling under the assault.

"Guardian One!" he shouted. "Guardian Two! Get to the first floor, now!"

The two golden constructs he'd created appeared at the meditation room's entrance—featureless faces, gleaming armor, spears raised. They saluted and vanished, teleporting to the audience hall above.

Wei Chen ran.

He burst through the meditation room's door and into the hallway that connected to the first floor. The tremors were worse here. The walls were warping, bulging inward as if something massive was pressing against them from the outside.

*The tribulation is targeting the core room. It knows where I am.*

"Then we don't let it get there," Wei Chen growled.

He reached the audience hall and stopped dead.

The ceiling was gone.

Not collapsed—gone. As if something had torn it away, revealing a sky that shouldn't exist. It wasn't the sky of the mountain. It wasn't any sky Wei Chen had ever seen. It was a churning mass of black clouds, shot through with veins of crimson lightning, swirling in a pattern that hurt to look at.

And at the center of that maelstrom, a single eye.

It was enormous—easily the size of the audience hall itself—and it was staring directly at him.

Wei Chen's blood turned to ice.

The eye was human in shape, but there was nothing human about it. The iris was a ring of fire, the pupil a void so deep it seemed to swallow light itself. And when it blinked, the world shuddered.

*Do not look at it directly. It will see into your soul.*

"Bit late for that advice," Wei Chen whispered, unable to tear his gaze away.

The eye focused on him. He felt it—a pressure, a weight, as if something was reaching into his chest and squeezing his heart. His knees buckled. His vision swam.

And then, a voice.

It didn't come through his ears. It came from inside his skull, from the marrow of his bones, from the deepest part of his being.

*You are not worthy.*

The words were ancient. They carried the weight of eons, of civilizations that had risen and fallen while the speaker watched from beyond the veil.

*The throne of Heaven is not for thieves. Not for grave robbers. Not for mortals who crawl through the dirt.*

Wei Chen forced himself to stand. His legs were shaking. His teeth were clenched so hard he thought they might crack.

"Funny," he managed, his voice barely more than a rasp. "The throne didn't seem to mind."

*The throne is broken. The core is damaged. It accepted you because it had no choice.*

"Then maybe I'll prove it wrong."

The eye narrowed. The pressure intensified. Wei Chen felt something warm trickling from his nose—blood.

*You are a worm pretending to be a dragon. I will burn your dungeon to ash. I will scatter your soul across the void. And I will take what is rightfully mine.*

"Yours?" Wei Chen spat blood. "Who the hell are you?"

The eye opened wider. The black clouds churned. And from the heart of the maelstrom, something began to descend.

It was a hand.

A hand of shadow and lightning, fingers each the size of a grown man, reaching down through the ruined ceiling toward the audience hall. The air screamed as it passed. The golden light on the walls guttered and died.

Guardian One leaped forward, spear raised.

The hand didn't even slow down. It swatted the celestial construct aside like a fly. Guardian One hit the far wall and shattered into golden fragments.

Guardian Two followed a moment later. Same result.

Wei Chen stood alone.

The hand descended toward him. He could feel its heat, its cold, its hunger. This wasn't just destruction—this was consumption. Whatever this thing was, it wanted to devour the dungeon, the core, everything.

*Wei Chen.* The core's voice was calm, almost peaceful. *I am sorry.*

"For what?"

*For dragging you into this. For giving you a burden you could not bear. You were right. You are not a god. You are a tomb raider. And I have doomed you.*

The hand was ten feet away. Five. Wei Chen could see the individual fingers, each one a claw of shadow, reaching for him.

He thought about running. He thought about hiding. He thought about all the tombs he'd raided, all the traps he'd escaped, all the times he'd cheated death.

And then he thought about the cultivators he'd met. The ones who came to his dungeon seeking power, seeking enlightenment, seeking to become something more than they were.

"You know what?" Wei Chen said, his voice steady now. "Maybe I am a worm. Maybe I'm not worthy. Maybe the core made a mistake."

The hand was inches away.

"But here's the thing about worms."

He reached into his pocket and pulled out the last thing he had—a fragment of the jade talisman he'd used to bind himself to the core. He'd kept it as a memento. A reminder of where he'd come from.

"We're good at surviving."

He crushed the fragment in his palm.

The jade shards cut into his flesh. Blood dripped onto the floor. And as the shadow hand closed around him, Wei Chen did something the core had never shown him, something he'd figured out on his own.

He poured every drop of his blood, every shred of his qi, every ounce of his will into the dungeon's formation arrays.

Not to strengthen them.

To overload them.

The audience hall exploded with light.

The shadow hand recoiled. The eye in the sky blinked, confused. And Wei Chen felt himself falling, falling, falling through layers of stone and earth and spiritual energy, until he landed hard on something soft and damp.

He opened his eyes.

He was in a cave. A natural cave, with rough stone walls and a ceiling dripping with moisture. The air smelled of wet earth and moss.

And in front of him, floating in the air, was the dungeon core.

*What did you do?*

Wei Chen coughed up blood. His body felt like it had been run over by a cart. Then backed over. Then run over again.

"I overloaded the arrays. Caused a massive spiritual explosion. It should buy us some time."

*The audience hall is destroyed. The first three floors are compromised. Years of growth, lost in moments.*

"Better than being dead."

The core pulsed. It didn't argue.

*The entity above. It is known to me now. I remember.*

"Who is it?"

*A general. One of the four Heavenly Generals who guarded the Jade Emperor's throne. He was the first to fall when the chaos came. The first to betray his oath.*

"Heavenly General... that's bad. That's really bad."

*It gets worse. He was not alone. There were four of them. If he has found us, the others will follow.*

Wei Chen closed his eyes. He was exhausted. Bleeding. His dungeon was in ruins. And a fallen god was trying to kill him.

"Great. Just great."

*We can rebuild. But we must be faster. Stronger. And we must find allies.*

"Allies? Who's going to help a tomb raider and a broken dungeon core?"

The core pulsed again. And this time, Wei Chen felt something new in its voice. Something that might have been hope.

*The cultivators who survive your dungeon. They will remember. They will speak of what they experienced. And some of them may return.*

"Return for what?"

*To help. To learn. To join.*

The word hung in the air, heavy with meaning.

*To become your first disciples.*

Wei Chen's eyes snapped open.

Above him, through the layers of stone and spiritual energy, he could still feel the eye watching. The shadow hand searching. The Heavenly General waiting.

But beneath that, in the darkness of the cave, the core pulsed with a steady, golden light.

And for the first time since he'd bound himself to the dungeon, Wei Chen felt something other than fear.

He felt determination.

"Alright," he said, pushing himself to his feet. "Let's rebuild."

The core showed him a new floor plan. A different approach. Not a palace this time. Not a hall of audience.

A labyrinth.

A maze of illusions and traps, designed not to impress, but to test. To filter. To find the ones who had potential.

Wei Chen looked at the plans.

He looked at the core.

And he grinned.

"We're going to need a lot more jade."

title: The Dragon King's Gambit
content:
The morning of the seventh day dawned grey and cold.

Wei Chen stood at the entrance of the Heavenly Dungeon, watching the mist curl around the broken pillars of the old celestial palace. Three days had passed since the first cultivators entered. Three days of successes and failures, of near-disasters and unexpected victories.

The core pulsed at his side—or rather, in his chest, where its presence had taken root like a second heart. *They are coming again. More this time.*

"I know." Wei Chen rubbed his temples. "I can feel them."

The past three days had been a brutal education. The first group of cultivators had been easy—Qi Condensation novices from a minor sect who had heard rumors of a "new ruin" in the forbidden mountains. They'd stumbled through the audience hall, tripped over his illusion formations, and fled screaming when Guardian One had emerged from the mist.

But word spread fast in the cultivation world.

By the second day, Foundation Establishment cultivators had arrived. They'd seen through the weaker illusions, battered Guardian One into scrap, and nearly reached the core chamber before Wei Chen had activated the emergency formations—a desperate gamble that had drained half his qi reserves.

Yesterday had been worse. A Nascent Soul elder from the Azure Cloud Sect had come personally, accompanied by a dozen disciples. The elder had laughed at Wei Chen's defenses, had casually shattered the reinforced pillars, had reached out with his spiritual sense to locate the core—

And then something had happened.

The core had *reacted*. Not to Wei Chen's command, but on its own. A wave of golden light had erupted from the floor, so pure and so ancient that the Nascent Soul elder had frozen in place. His face had gone pale. He'd whispered something Wei Chen couldn't hear, then turned and walked out without a word, his disciples scrambling after him.

The other cultivators had followed. Within an hour, the dungeon was empty.

Wei Chen still didn't understand what had happened. The core had gone quiet afterward, its pulses weak and irregular, as if the effort had cost it dearly.

*They will return today,* the core sent, its voice thinner than before. *The elder will return. And he will bring others.*

"Great." Wei Chen kicked a loose stone. "So I've got, what, a day? Maybe two?"

*Less. The barrier I created will not hold. He is analyzing it even now.*

"Then what do I do?"

The core was silent.

"You don't know, do you?" Wei Chen laughed bitterly. "The great Heavenly Dungeon Core, and it's got nothing."

*I have memories. Fragments. The old Heavenly Court had enemies. Dark forces that consumed the gods. But the method of their destruction is... unclear.*

"Unclear. Perfect." Wei Chen started pacing. "So we're blind. We're weak. And in a few hours, a Nascent Soul monster is going to tear through my defenses and take you."

*Not necessarily.*

Wei Chen stopped. "What do you mean?"

The core pulsed, and an image formed in his mind—a map, stretching deep beneath the mountains. Tunnels and chambers, some natural, some carved with purpose. At the very bottom, miles below the surface, something gleamed.

*The old Heavenly Court had allies. Those who fled before the fall. They left behind armories, treasuries, sanctuaries. One of them lies beneath us.*

"And you're just telling me this now?"

*I did not remember until the elder's attack. His qi triggered something.*

Wei Chen stared at the image. The path was long and treacherous, winding through unstable earth and flooded caverns. But at the end—

"What's down there?"

*A Dragon King's hoard. One of the Four Dragon Kings of the Four Seas. He was the first to fall, but his treasures were hidden before the darkness reached him.*

Dragon King. The words sent a chill down Wei Chen's spine. In the old myths, the Dragon Kings were among the most powerful beings in existence—masters of storms and tides, commanders of armies, rulers of the seas. If even one of them had fallen...

*The hoard will contain weapons. Formations. Resources. Enough to strengthen the dungeon. Enough to survive.*

"And how do I get there without the Nascent Soul elder following me?"

*He will not follow. The path is sealed to those without the core's blessing. But you must go alone. The journey will take days. The dungeon will be vulnerable.*

Wei Chen laughed. "So my options are: stay here and die in a few hours, or leave the dungeon defenseless and hope I find a miracle before anyone breaks in."

*Yes.*

"Great choices. Really inspiring."

But even as he said it, Wei Chen was moving. He grabbed a pack from the corner of the audience hall, stuffed it with the few supplies he had—dried meat, water, a compass that pointed toward spiritual energy. He checked the illusion formations one last time, reinforced them with a trickle of qi, and placed Guardian One at the entrance.

"If anyone comes," he told the blank-faced construct, "do your best. Scream loud. Make them think there's more of you."

The construct nodded once.

Wei Chen turned and walked toward the back of the audience hall, where a hidden passage had opened during the night—a narrow staircase spiraling down into darkness. The core pulsed, and the stairs gleamed with faint golden light, guiding his way.

"Here goes nothing."

He descended.

---

The first hour was silent.

The staircase wound deeper than Wei Chen had expected, past walls carved with ancient runes he couldn't read, past chambers filled with the bones of creatures he couldn't identify. The air grew thick and heavy, pressing against his ears, making his head ache.

The second hour brought sounds.

Whispers, at first. Faint voices that seemed to come from the walls themselves, speaking in a language that was old and wrong. Wei Chen tried to ignore them, but they grew louder as he descended, until they were almost screaming, a cacophony of rage and grief and madness.

*Do not listen,* the core sent. *These are echoes of the old war. The darkness left its mark.*

"Easy for you to say. You don't have ears."

But he pressed on, covering his ears with his hands, focusing on the golden light ahead.

The third hour brought the first trial.

The staircase ended at a vast underground lake, its waters black and still. The golden light showed him a path—a narrow bridge of stone that stretched across the surface, disappearing into darkness on the far side.

Wei Chen stepped onto the bridge.

The whispers stopped.

The water beneath him began to churn.

Something rose from the depths—a shape, massive and serpentine, with eyes like burning coals. It circled once, twice, then stopped directly in front of him, blocking his path.

"You carry the core," it said, its voice like grinding stone. "The key to the Heavenly Court. The last hope of the fallen gods."

Wei Chen's hand went to his chest, where the core pulsed faster. "Who are you?"

"An echo. A remnant. A guardian of the Dragon King's passage." The creature's eyes narrowed. "You are not worthy."

"I don't have time to be worthy. I need to reach the hoard."

"Many have tried. All have failed. The Dragon King's treasures are not for the unworthy."

Wei Chen's jaw tightened. "Then what do I have to do?"

The creature laughed—a sound like rocks grinding together. "Prove yourself. Face the trial of the Dragon King. Survive."

Golden light exploded from the water, and Wei Chen felt himself falling.

---

He landed hard on stone, the breath knocked from his lungs.

When he opened his eyes, he was no longer in the cave. He was standing in a vast hall, its ceiling lost in mist, its walls lined with tapestries depicting wars and storms and dragons in flight. At the far end, on a throne of coral and pearl, sat a figure.

The Dragon King.

He was enormous—easily three times the height of a man—with scales of deep blue and green, a mane of white hair that flowed like water, and eyes that held the weight of ages. He wore armor of jade and gold, and in his hand he held a spear that crackled with lightning.

"So," the Dragon King said, his voice echoing through the hall, "the core has chosen a new master. A tomb raider. A thief of graves."

Wei Chen's mouth went dry. "I prefer 'archaeologist.'"

The Dragon King's lips curled. "You have nerve. I will give you that." He rose from his throne, and the ground trembled. "But nerve is not enough. The darkness that consumed the Heavenly Court will consume you as well. Unless you can prove yourself worthy of the treasures I left behind."

"How do I do that?"

"Fight me."

Wei Chen stared. "Fight you. The Dragon King. One of the most powerful beings in existence."

"An echo of me. A fraction of my true power. But enough to test you." The Dragon King raised his spear. "Survive for three minutes, and I will grant you access to my hoard."

"And if I don't?"

"You will die. Your soul will join the echoes in the walls. And the dungeon will fall to the darkness."

Wei Chen took a breath. Then another.

He had no weapon. No armor. No cultivation to speak of.

But he had the core.

*Can you help me?* he asked silently.

The core pulsed. *I can lend you my power. But it will be painful. And it will drain me further.*

*Do it.*

Golden light flooded his veins. He felt the core's energy surge through him, burning and cold at the same time, filling every cell with power he could barely contain. His vision sharpened. His muscles tightened. The world seemed to slow.

The Dragon King smiled. "Interesting. You have more fire than I expected."

He lunged.

Wei Chen dove to the side, the spear passing inches from his face, the wind of its passage cutting his cheek. He rolled, came up, and threw a punch—a wild, desperate strike that connected with the Dragon King's armor and did nothing but bruise his hand.

"Pathetic," the Dragon King said, and brought the spear around in a sweeping arc.

Wei Chen jumped, barely clearing the blade. He landed off-balance, stumbled, and felt the Dragon King's tail catch him across the chest, sending him flying into a pillar.

The impact cracked ribs. He tasted blood.

*One minute,* the core sent. *You have survived one minute.*

"Great," Wei Chen gasped, pulling himself to his feet. "Only two more to go."

The Dragon King was already moving again, his spear a blur of light and death. Wei Chen dodged, ducked, weaved—but he was slowing, the core's energy fading, his body screaming in protest.

He couldn't keep this up.

He needed something else. Something the Dragon King wouldn't expect.

And then he remembered.

The illusion formations.

He wasn't a fighter. He was a tomb raider. A trickster. A man who had survived for years by being clever, not strong.

He reached into the core, pulled out a thread of its energy, and wove it into a pattern—the same pattern he'd used in the audience hall, but twisted, warped, made more aggressive.

The Dragon King's spear came down—

And struck empty air.

Because Wei Chen was no longer there.

He was standing behind the Dragon King, his hands weaving faster, the illusion spreading like a web. The Dragon King turned, confused, and saw not one Wei Chen but a dozen, all moving

title: The Eighth Floor's Secret
content:
The morning light filtered through the clouds like spilled honey, golden and thick, as Wei Chen stood at the entrance of his dungeon. Three weeks had passed since the first cultivators arrived. Three weeks of near-constant battle, of refining, of watching his dungeon grow from a single pathetic hall into something that actually resembled a trial ground.

Seven floors now stood beneath the Jade Emperor's palace. Seven floors of increasingly complex challenges, each one designed to test, to reward, to tempt cultivators deeper into his domain. The eighth floor was taking shape in his mind, a labyrinth of mirrors and misdirection that would make even Nascent Soul experts think twice.

But that wasn't what had him standing at the entrance at dawn.

Something was wrong.

The qi flowing through the dungeon had changed. It was subtle—barely noticeable unless you were attuned to the core's every pulse—but it was there. A discordant note in an otherwise perfect symphony. Like a single string on a guqin tuned slightly off-key.

*The cultivators from yesterday,* the core pulsed. *The woman with the jade hairpin.*

Wei Chen's jaw tightened. He remembered her. Late Foundation Establishment stage, quiet, unassuming. She'd completed the third floor, taken her reward, and left without incident. Nothing about her had stood out.

That was precisely what bothered him.

"Show me her path," he said.

The core obliged. Images flooded his mind—the woman's journey through the dungeon, her interactions with the guardians, the way she'd paused at certain formations. Not randomly. Deliberately. As if she were reading something he hadn't written.

*She touched the pillars on the fourth floor. Nine times. In sequence.*

"A formation key," Wei Chen muttered. "Someone taught her how to read my arrays."

*Worse.* The core's pulse was grim. *She left something behind.*

He felt it before he saw it—a cold spot in the qi network, like a bruise on living flesh. He traced it to the fifth floor, to a shadowed alcove behind the waterfall formation. There, wedged between two stones, was a small black talisman.

It wasn't his.

The moment his fingers brushed it, the talisman dissolved into smoke. But the smoke didn't dissipate. It coalesced, forming a face—gaunt, ancient, with eyes like dying stars.

"So," the face said, its voice dry as bone dust, "the little tomb raider finally noticed."

Wei Chen didn't flinch. "Who are you?"

"An old friend of the Heavenly Court. Or perhaps an old enemy. The line blurs after ten thousand years." The smoke-face smiled, and it was not a pleasant expression. "I've been watching you, Wei Chen. Watching your little dungeon grow. You've done well, for a mortal. Better than I expected."

"You've been spying on me."

"Of course. The Heavenly Dungeon Core is the most valuable treasure in the Three Realms. Did you think you were the only one who knew it had awakened?" The face's eyes narrowed. "There are others, boy. Others who remember the old days. Others who want what you have."

Wei Chen's hand drifted to the core fragment he kept in his pocket. "Then why haven't they taken it?"

"Because they're afraid. The core chose you—that means something, even to the most power-hungry among us. To claim it by force would risk destroying it." The smoke-face chuckled. "But there are other ways to claim a treasure. Patience. Subtlety. Infiltration."

"The woman with the jade hairpin."

"One of many. I have agents in every sect, every city, every corner of the cultivation world. They watch. They wait. They report." The face tilted, studying him. "And they plant seeds. That talisman was the twelfth I've placed in your dungeon this week. You only found one."

Twelve. Wei Chen's blood ran cold. He'd been so focused on building, on defending against obvious attacks, that he'd missed the insidious spread of espionage within his own walls.

"What do you want?"

"Nothing you'll give willingly. But I'm a patient man. I've waited ten thousand years. I can wait a little longer." The smoke began to fade. "Enjoy your dungeon, little tomb raider. Enjoy it while it's still yours."

"No," Wei Chen said. "I don't think so."

He grabbed the smoke with his bare hand—a stupid, reckless move—and *pulled*. The core screamed in his mind, but he ignored it. He'd learned something in the past three weeks, something the old Heavenly Court had forgotten.

A dungeon wasn't just stone and formations. It was *will*. The will of its master, crystallized into reality.

And right now, his will was iron.

The smoke fought him, writhing and twisting, but he held on. He dragged it back from dissolution, forced it to remain, to take solid form. The face screamed, its composure cracking for the first time.

"What—how are you—"

"I'm the dungeon master," Wei Chen said, his voice flat. "And in my dungeon, nothing enters or leaves without my permission."

He crushed the smoke in his fist.

The scream cut off instantly. When he opened his hand, a single black crystal sat in his palm, pulsing with trapped qi. A piece of his enemy, captured and contained.

The core pulsed with alarm. *Wei Chen. The backlash—*

"I know." He could feel it—a ripple of disturbance spreading through the dungeon's foundations, cracks forming in places he'd thought unbreakable. Using his will like that had cost him. Cost the dungeon. "But I couldn't let him go. He knows too much."

*He knows nothing now. You sealed his connection to that fragment.*

"For now." Wei Chen pocketed the crystal. "But there are eleven more. And he'll know I found this one. He'll adapt."

*Then we must adapt faster.*

Wei Chen turned and walked deeper into the dungeon, his footsteps echoing off jade walls. Seven floors of carefully crafted trials. Hundreds of formations. Dozens of guardians. And none of it would matter if he couldn't root out the spies in his own house.

He stopped at the seventh floor's central chamber. The ceiling opened to a simulated sky, stars wheeling in an endless dance. The floor was mirror-smooth obsidian, reflecting everything with perfect clarity.

"Show me," he said. "Show me every foreign qi signature in the dungeon."

The core hesitated. *There are... many. The cultivators who came through yesterday—*

"Not them. Hidden ones. Talismans, artifacts, anything that doesn't belong."

The floor rippled. Slowly, painfully slowly, points of light began to appear in the obsidian. Red lights. Intruder lights.

One. Two. Three. Four. Five.

By the time they stopped, Wei Chen was staring at forty-seven red dots scattered across his dungeon's seven floors.

Forty-seven.

"Twelve talismans, he said. The liar." Wei Chen's voice was cold. "He's been planting these since day one."

*We can purge them. One by one.*

"Too slow. He'll know we're onto him. He'll accelerate whatever plan he has." Wei Chen paced, his mind racing. "No. We need to turn this against him. Leave the talismans in place. Let him think we're blind."

*And when he activates them?*

"Then we'll be ready." Wei Chen stopped pacing. "But first, I need to know who he is. The crystal I captured—can you trace its origin?"

Another hesitation. Longer this time. *The qi signature is... ancient. Older than the Heavenly Court's fall. It bears the mark of the Primordial Chaos.*

Wei Chen's blood ran cold. "Primordial Chaos? As in, before the gods?"

*Yes. The being you faced was not a cultivator. It was something older. Something that existed before the Jade Emperor established order.*

"Then why does it want my dungeon?"

*Because,* the core said slowly, *it created the dungeon. In a way. The Heavenly Court was built on the ruins of something that came before. Something the Jade Emperor defeated and sealed away.*

The floor beneath Wei Chen's feet trembled.

*Wei Chen. The eighth floor.*

"What about it?"

*I didn't build it.*

Wei Chen's heart stopped. "What do you mean you didn't build it?"

*The eighth floor. I thought it was my own design—a natural evolution of the dungeon's growth. But now... I see the truth. The formations, the layout, the qi patterns... They're not mine. They were hidden in the core's memory, dormant, waiting to be activated.*

"Activated by what?"

*By the talismans. The forty-seven points of foreign qi. They're not just spies. They're keys. A lock I didn't know existed.*

The floor trembled again, harder this time. Cracks spider-webbed across the obsidian.

*The eighth floor is opening. And I can't stop it.*

Wei Chen ran.

He sprinted through the dungeon, past startled guardians and half-formed constructs, down stairs that twisted in ways they shouldn't, through corridors that rearranged themselves as he passed. The dungeon was changing, warping, and not in a way he controlled.

He reached the entrance to the eighth floor just as it finished forming.

It wasn't a labyrinth of mirrors.

It was a throne room.

A throne of black jade sat at the far end, massive and imposing, carved with symbols so ancient they hurt to look at. The walls were covered in murals—battles, conquests, the rise and fall of civilizations. And at the center of it all, a figure that made Wei Chen's blood freeze.

Not a god. Not a demon.

A shadow. A void in the shape of a man, seated on the throne as if it had always been his.

It turned its head. No—it *turned* in his direction, though it had no head to turn.

"Welcome," it said, and the voice was the sound of a thousand screams silenced. "I've been waiting."

The forty-seven talismans activated simultaneously.

The dungeon screamed.

Wei Chen fell to his knees, clutching his head as the core's agony flooded through their connection. The shadow rose from the throne, drifting toward him like smoke on a battlefield.

"You thought you were building a new Heavenly Court," it said. "But you were only rebuilding my prison."

The eighth floor's walls began to close in.

And somewhere, far below, something ancient and terrible began to wake.

title: The Tribulation of Flowing Sand
content:
The morning of the seventh day dawned gray and heavy over Mount Kunlun.

Wei Chen stood at the entrance of the Heavenly Dungeon, watching the clouds roll in from the east. They moved wrong—too fast, too deliberate, like a herd of panicked cattle fleeing something unseen. The wind carried a scent he didn't recognize. Old. Ancient. Like dust from a tomb that had been sealed since before the First Emperor.

"You feel that?" he asked.

Guardian One stood beside him, still featureless, still silent. But its spear had begun to hum—a low, resonant note that vibrated through the stone floor.

The core pulsed in his mind. *Danger approaches.*

"No shit."

Wei Chen had spent the past three days expanding the dungeon. The first floor now had three proper chambers: the Celestial Audience Hall, the Meditation Room, and a new addition he'd called the Hall of Reflections. The illusions were in place, layered so thick that even he sometimes forgot which parts were real. Guardian One had been joined by Guardian Two and Guardian Three, each identical, each waiting for a soul to fill its empty shell.

It wasn't enough. He knew it wasn't enough.

But it would have to do.

The first cultivators arrived at noon.

There were five of them. They walked up the mountain path with the easy confidence of people who had never known real danger. The leader was a man in his forties, wearing robes of blue silk embroidered with silver clouds. His qi burned bright and steady—Core Formation realm, if Wei Chen had to guess. The others were younger, probably his disciples. Qi Condensation, maybe one at Foundation Establishment.

"Greetings," the leader said, stopping twenty feet from the dungeon entrance. His eyes swept over the stone archway, the golden light pulsing from within, the three guardians standing motionless at attention. "I am Elder Zhao of the Azure Cloud Sect. We have heard rumors of a new dungeon on Mount Kunlun. We have come to test its depths."

Wei Chen stepped forward, keeping his posture relaxed. He'd spent the morning practicing his immortal demeanor. "You are welcome, Elder Zhao. The Heavenly Dungeon opens its doors to all who seek cultivation."

Elder Zhao's eyes narrowed. "Heavenly Dungeon? Bold name for an unranked dungeon."

"Bold times require bold names."

The disciples behind Elder Zhao exchanged glances. One of them—a young woman with sharp features and a sword at her hip—snorted. "Bold or foolish? There's a difference."

"Yue," Elder Zhao said, without turning. "Respect."

She fell silent, but her eyes never left Wei Chen. They were assessing him, measuring him, finding him lacking.

*She's not wrong,* Wei Chen thought. *I am lacking. But I'm learning.*

The core pulsed. *Let them enter.*

Wei Chen stepped aside. "The dungeon awaits."

Elder Zhao led his disciples through the archway. The golden light swallowed them whole.

Wei Chen followed at a distance, watching through the dungeon's eyes.

The Celestial Audience Hall unfolded before them in all its reconstructed glory. The golden pillars rose into mist. The jade floor gleamed. The murals on the walls showed scenes of ancient heavenly battles—gods and demons clashing, stars falling from the sky, the Jade Emperor seated on his throne with a sword of pure light in his hand.

The disciples stared, their arrogance momentarily forgotten.

"It's beautiful," one of them whispered.

"It's a trap," Yue said, her hand on her sword. "Don't let your guard down."

Elder Zhao nodded approvingly. "Well said, Yue. But not everything that shines is dangerous." He walked forward, his robes brushing the jade floor. "The formation arrays here are... interesting. They're not hostile. They're nourishing."

"Father?" Yue's voice cracked with surprise. "You mean—"

"The qi in this chamber is more refined than anything in our sect's cultivation grounds." Elder Zhao turned in a slow circle, his eyes bright. "Whoever built this dungeon knows what they're doing."

*If only you knew,* Wei Chen thought, hiding a smile.

The first test came in the Hall of Reflections.

Elder Zhao walked through the archway and stopped. The room was empty—or seemed empty. Mirrors lined every wall, reflecting his image back at him from a hundred different angles. But the reflections weren't identical. Some showed him younger. Some showed him older. One showed him with a sword through his chest.

"An illusion array," Elder Zhao murmured. "Clever."

Wei Chen watched from the core chamber, his consciousness spread through the dungeon like a spider's web. The Hall of Reflections was his masterpiece—a maze of mirrors and qi that forced cultivators to confront their own weaknesses. It didn't attack. It revealed.

Elder Zhao walked deeper into the hall. His reflections shifted, changed, showed him memories he had buried. His first defeat. His greatest failure. The face of a woman he had loved and lost.

For a moment, the Core Formation elder faltered. His qi flickered.

Then he laughed.

"Is that all?" He spread his arms wide. "I have lived four hundred years. I have known loss and pain and regret. These shadows cannot touch me."

He walked through the mirrors as if they weren't there, and they shattered behind him, scattering light across the floor.

The disciples followed, each struggling in their own way. One broke down crying. Another punched a mirror and cut his hand. But Yue walked through without breaking stride, her eyes fixed forward, her sword unsheathed but unused.

She passed through the hall and emerged into the Celestial Audience Hall's second chamber, where Guardian One waited.

"You're not real," she said.

The guardian raised its spear.

Yue moved.

Wei Chen had never seen anyone fight like her. She was fast—impossibly fast—her sword tracing arcs of silver light that cut through the guardian's golden armor like paper. Guardian One struck back, its spear thrusting with the weight of a thousand years of celestial warfare, but Yue was already somewhere else, always somewhere else.

In three seconds, it was over.

Guardian One dissolved into golden light, its core shattered.

Yue stood in the center of the chamber, breathing hard, her sword dripping with spiritual energy. "I told you," she said, looking directly at where Wei Chen was watching. "Not real."

Elder Zhao emerged from the Hall of Reflections, his robes unruffled, his smile intact. "Excellent work, Yue. You've cleared the first floor."

"There's more," Yue said. "I can feel it. Deeper."

"There's always more." Elder Zhao turned to face the wall, where a new passage had opened—a staircase leading down into darkness. "Shall we?"

Wei Chen's blood ran cold.

He hadn't built a second floor.

The staircase wasn't his.

*Core,* he sent urgently. *What is that?*

The core's pulse was slow, heavy, like the heartbeat of something waking from a long sleep. *It is... not ours.*

*Then whose is it?*

*I do not know.*

Elder Zhao descended the staircase without hesitation. His disciples followed, Yue at the rear, her sword still drawn.

Wei Chen pulled his consciousness back, abandoning the observation, and ran.

He reached the staircase just as Yue was about to descend. "Wait!"

She turned, her sword rising. "You. The dungeon master."

"Don't go down there."

"Why not?"

Because I don't know what's down there. Because I didn't build it. Because something is wrong.

"Because the dungeon isn't ready," he said instead. "The lower floors are unstable."

Yue's eyes narrowed. "You're lying."

"I'm not."

"You're a terrible liar." She stepped onto the first stair. "And I don't trust you."

She descended.

Wei Chen cursed and followed.

The staircase was long—much longer than it should have been. The walls were rough stone, not the polished jade of the first floor. The air grew cold, then hot, then cold again. Strange symbols were carved into the walls, symbols that made Wei Chen's eyes water when he tried to look at them directly.

*Core,* he sent. *Do you recognize these symbols?*

A long pause. Then: *Yes. They are from the age before the Heavenly Court. The age of the Primordial Chaos.*

*That's bad, isn't it?*

*Very.*

The staircase ended in a chamber that should not exist.

It was vast—vaster than the Celestial Audience Hall, vaster than anything Wei Chen had built. The ceiling was lost in darkness. The floor was black sand, shifting and whispering as if alive. And in the center of the chamber, half-buried in the sand, was a mountain of bones.

Human bones. Animal bones. Bones that did not belong to anything Wei Chen recognized.

And on top of the mountain, sitting cross-legged as if waiting, was a figure wrapped in tattered yellow robes.

"Welcome," the figure said, its voice like grinding stones. "I was wondering when the new master would find my little addition."

Elder Zhao stood at the edge of the black sand, his hand raised to stop his disciples. "Who are you?"

"Me?" The figure stood, bones crunching beneath its feet. Yellow cloth fell away to reveal withered skin stretched tight over a skeleton's frame. "I am the last keeper of the Flowing Sand River. The guardian of the boundary between heaven and earth. The one who watched when the Heavenly Court fell." Its eyes—two points of pale light in hollow sockets—fixed on Wei Chen. "And I am the one who will decide if you are worthy to rebuild it."

Wei Chen's heart stopped.

"You have seven days," the figure said. "Seven days to reach the bottom of the Flowing Sand River and claim the treasure that lies there. If you succeed, I will acknowledge you as the new Jade Emperor's steward. If you fail..." It smiled, and its teeth were needle-sharp. "Then I will add your bones to my collection."

The black sand began to churn.

And the floor opened beneath them all.

title: The Heavenly Messenger's Warning
content:
Wei Chen stood in the heart of the Celestial Audience Hall, his fingers tracing the intricate patterns of the illusion formations he'd spent the past three days perfecting. The golden light pulsed in rhythm with his breathing, and he could feel the dungeon's approval washing over him like a warm tide.

Three days of preparation.

Three days of sleepless work, of feeding the core everything he had, of learning techniques that would have taken mortal cultivators decades to master.

And now, the first floor was complete.

The meditation room hummed with concentrated qi, its formations ready to help any cultivator who sat within its bounds. The Celestial Audience Hall stretched before him, majestic and imposing, its murals now glowing with barely suppressed power. And beneath it all, a web of illusionary death waited.

Guardian One stood at attention, his featureless face turned toward the entrance. Wei Chen had spent the last hour reinforcing the construct, pouring additional qi into its frame until it could at least put up a fight against a mid-stage Qi Condensation cultivator.

It wasn't much. But it was something.

"Alright," Wei Chen said, rolling his shoulders. "Let's see if this works."

He stepped back to the center of the hall and closed his eyes. The core pulsed beneath him, and he reached out with his spiritual sense, activating the illusion formations.

The hall shifted.

To his enhanced senses, nothing had changed. The pillars still stood, the floor still gleamed, the mist still swirled overhead. But to anyone without his connection to the core, the hall would now appear as a labyrinth of identical corridors, each one leading to a dead end or a trap.

It wasn't perfect. A sharp-eyed cultivator might notice the inconsistencies—the way the light bent wrong, the way the shadows didn't quite match the pillars. But for a first attempt, it was damned impressive.

"Not bad," Wei Chen said, allowing himself a small smile. "Not bad at all."

The core pulsed with approval.

And then something changed.

Wei Chen felt it before he saw it—a ripple in the fabric of the dungeon's domain, like a stone thrown into a still pond. The core flared with alarm, and his spiritual sense snapped outward, searching for the source.

Someone was approaching.

No. Not approaching. *Arriving.*

The air in front of him split open, a crack of silver light that widened into a portal. Wei Chen's hand went to the jade dagger at his belt, his heart hammering in his chest. The portal was elegant, refined—nothing like the crude spatial tears he'd seen mortal cultivators use.

This was celestial magic.

A figure stepped through.

She was tall, with skin like porcelain and hair that flowed like liquid night. Her robes were white, embroidered with silver clouds, and her eyes held the cold, distant light of stars. In her hand, she carried a scroll bound with golden thread.

Wei Chen's blood ran cold.

He'd seen robes like that before. In paintings. In old texts. In the fragments of memories the core had shown him.

*Heavenly Court attire.*

The woman's gaze swept the hall, taking in the murals, the pillars, the mist. Her expression was unreadable, but Wei Chen caught the faintest flicker of something—surprise? Recognition?—before it vanished.

"So," she said, her voice like wind chimes in a storm. "It's true."

Wei Chen forced himself to relax, to keep his hand away from his dagger. "I'm sorry," he said, putting on his best confused expression. "Do I know you?"

The woman's lips curled into something that might have been a smile. "You're Wei Chen. Tomb raider. Thief. The one who stumbled into the Heavenly Dungeon and bound himself to its core."

*Shit.*

"How do you know that?"

"I know many things." She stepped forward, and Wei Chen felt the dungeon's defenses stir. Guardian One shifted, his spear lowering into a defensive position. The woman glanced at the construct, then back at Wei Chen. "Your guardian is weak. A Qi Condensation cultivator could destroy it with a single technique."

"I'm working on it."

"Not fast enough."

Wei Chen's jaw tightened. "Who are you? And why are you here?"

The woman held up the scroll. "I am Yue Ling, former Archivist of the Heavenly Court. I was sent by the remnants of the celestial bureaucracy to deliver a message."

"Remnants?" Wei Chen's eyes narrowed. "I thought the Heavenly Court had fallen."

"It has. But not all of us perished." Yue Ling's voice carried a weight of centuries. "Some of us survived. We hid. We waited. And we watched."

"Watched for what?"

"For you."

The words hung in the air like a guillotine blade. Wei Chen's mind raced, trying to process what she was saying. The core pulsed beneath him, and he felt its confusion mirroring his own.

"I don't understand," he said. "How did you know I was here? How did you find this place?"

Yue Ling's eyes softened, just slightly. "The Heavenly Dungeon was not meant to be hidden. It was meant to be found. By the right person." She paused. "And when the core bonded with a new master, the remnants felt it. A ripple across the celestial plane."

"So you came to congratulate me?"

"I came to warn you."

The playfulness vanished from her voice, replaced by something cold and hard. She unrolled the scroll, and Wei Chen saw characters written in golden ink—ancient script that pulsed with power.

"The dark forces that destroyed the old gods," Yue Ling said, "did not simply vanish. They have been waiting. Growing. Preparing." Her finger traced a line of text. "And now they know about you."

Wei Chen's stomach dropped. "How?"

"The same ripple that reached me reached them. They have their seers, their diviners, their methods of spying on the celestial currents." She rolled the scroll back up. "They know that a new Jade Emperor is being forged. And they will not allow it."

"How long do I have?"

"A month. Perhaps less." Yue Ling's gaze was steady. "They will send assassins. Powerful ones. Cultivators who have spent centuries perfecting their arts, who have made pacts with dark entities beyond mortal comprehension."

Wei Chen laughed—a short, bitter sound. "A month. I've been the dungeon master for three days."

"Then you had better use that month wisely."

She turned toward the portal, which still shimmered at the edge of the hall. But before she stepped through, she paused.

"I have one more thing to tell you, Wei Chen." Her voice softened, and for a moment, she looked almost human. "The remnants of the Heavenly Court are divided. Some see you as a threat. Others see you as a tool. And a very few—a very few see you as hope."

"What do you see me as?"

Yue Ling was silent for a long moment. Then she said, "I see a man who is in over his head. But I also see a man who refused to give up when the core showed him the truth." She smiled, and it was almost warm. "Perhaps that is enough."

She stepped through the portal, and it closed behind her with a whisper of silver light.

Wei Chen stood alone in the Celestial Audience Hall, his heart pounding, his mind racing.

A month.

He had a month to prepare for an attack from forces that had destroyed the old gods.

He looked down at his hands. They were shaking.

"Core," he said, his voice hoarse. "Can we do this?"

The core pulsed beneath him, and he felt its answer. It was not certainty. It was not confidence.

It was *determination.*

*We must try.*

Wei Chen took a deep breath, steadying himself. Then he turned and walked toward the entrance of the hall.

"Guardian One," he said. "With me."

The construct fell into step behind him, its blank face somehow conveying a sense of loyalty.

Wei Chen didn't know what the future held. He didn't know if he could survive against enemies that had toppled celestial empires.

But he knew one thing.

He was not going down without a fight.

---

An hour later, Wei Chen sat in the meditation room, the formation beneath him glowing softly as he refined his qi. The core had shown him a technique for accelerating cultivation, and he was desperate enough to try anything.

The door to the meditation room slid open.

Wei Chen's eyes snapped open, his hand reaching for his dagger. But it wasn't an enemy.

It was a woman.

She was young, perhaps in her early twenties, with sharp eyes and a scar running down her left cheek. Her robes were travel-worn, her boots caked with mud, and she carried a sword at her hip.

She looked around the meditation room with open curiosity.

"Nice place," she said. "Bit empty, though."

Wei Chen stared at her. "Who are you? How did you get in?"

The woman grinned. "I'm a cultivator. And I'm here for the dungeon."

Wei Chen's blood ran cold.

*The cultivators.*

He'd forgotten. He'd been so focused on the celestial threat that he'd forgotten about the original plan—to attract mortal cultivators as adventurers, to help them grow stronger, to harvest the qi they generated.

But this woman didn't look like an adventurer.

She looked like a hunter.

"I'm Lin Mei," she said, stepping further into the room. "Qi Condensation, seventh stage. I heard rumors about a new dungeon appearing in the mountains. Thought I'd check it out." She tilted her head, studying him. "You're the dungeon master?"

Wei Chen's mind raced. He could activate the illusions, try to trap her. But his instincts told him to be careful.

"Yes," he said, slowly rising to his feet. "I am."

Lin Mei's grin widened. "Good. Then you can tell me—what's the challenge? What do I have to do to earn the rewards?"

Wei Chen blinked.

"Rewards?"

"Of course." She laughed. "Every dungeon has rewards. Techniques. Treasures. Qi-enriched pills." Her eyes gleamed. "What do you have?"

Wei Chen opened his mouth to answer.

And then the core pulsed with sudden, violent urgency.

*Enemy detected.*

*Multiple signatures.*

*Approaching fast.*

Wei Chen's blood turned to ice.

The assassins.

They had come early.

title: The Ninth Floor Breaks
content:
The scream echoed through the dungeon's foundations, rattling the jade tiles beneath Wei Chen's feet. He dropped the formation brush, chalk scattering across the half-finished array he'd been carving into the eighth floor's eastern wall.

"What was that?"

Guardian Seven—now a proper celestial soldier with a face carved from marble and eyes that held faint starlight—turned its head toward the central shaft. "The scream came from below, Master. Deep below."

Wei Chen's blood ran cold. Below meant the ninth floor. The floor he hadn't touched yet. The floor that had been sealed since before he'd bound himself to the core.

*It cannot be.*

The core's voice was different now. Not the warm, guiding presence he'd grown accustomed to over the past weeks. This was sharp. Panicked. Ancient fear bleeding through the connection.

*The seal. Something has breached the seal.*

"We sealed it with nine layers of formation arrays," Wei Chen said, already running toward the spiral staircase that led downward. "I reinforced it myself last week. Nothing should be able to—"

Another scream. This one cut off mid-cry, replaced by a wet, crunching sound that made Wei Chen's stomach lurch.

He took the stairs three at a time, Guardian Seven clanking behind him in full armor. The dungeon's qi had changed. Where before it had been warm and nourishing, now it carried an undertone of rot—something old and hungry pressing against the edges of his domain.

The ninth floor entrance was a mess.

The jade door that had sealed the staircase lay in fragments, its surface blackened and cracked. The formation arrays he'd so carefully inscribed were gone, erased as if by acid. And the air that rose from the darkness below smelled of copper and decay.

"Master, I must advise against proceeding," Guardian Seven said, its voice flat and mechanical. "The readings from below indicate a threat level beyond our current capabilities."

"Noted." Wei Chen stepped through the broken doorway.

The staircase descended into absolute darkness. Not the natural darkness of a cave or a moonless night—this was a living darkness, thick and oily, that seemed to press against his skin. His spiritual sense, which had grown sharp over the weeks of feeding the dungeon, could only penetrate a few feet ahead.

He reached the bottom.

The ninth floor had changed.

When he'd first bound himself to the core, he'd glimpsed this level through the dungeon's memories. It had been a prison then—a series of crystalline cells designed to hold the most dangerous beings the Heavenly Court had ever captured. Gods who had fallen. Demons who had refused to kneel. And one other thing. Something the core refused to show him clearly.

Now the cells were empty. Their doors hung open, hinges twisted, locks shattered.

And in the center of the floor, where the main corridor opened into a vast chamber, something waited.

Wei Chen stopped breathing.

It looked human. Almost. A woman, perhaps, with long black hair that moved despite the still air and skin so pale it seemed to glow in the darkness. She wore robes of deepest crimson, embroidered with characters that hurt to look at—ancient script that predated even the Heavenly Court.

She was smiling.

"You're early," she said. Her voice was beautiful. Melodic. The kind of voice that made you want to listen forever. "I was told the new Jade Emperor would take at least a century to find his way down here."

Wei Chen's hand moved instinctively to the jade token at his throat. "Who are you?"

"I'm offended you don't recognize me." She stepped forward, and the darkness seemed to flow around her like a living thing. "I was the first prisoner. The one they built this dungeon to contain. The one even the old gods feared to speak of."

*The Crimson Concubine.*

The core's voice was barely a whisper now, laced with terror.

*She was the Jade Emperor's greatest failure. His consort. His betrayer. He could not kill her. He could only trap her.*

"Ah, so the core remembers." The Crimson Concubine's smile widened. "I wondered how much of its memory had survived the fall. Not much, apparently, or you would never have come down here alone."

Wei Chen's mind raced. He'd been building the dungeon for weeks, floor by floor, guardian by guardian. He'd attracted cultivators, repelled invaders, grown stronger with each passing day. But he'd been so focused on building upward that he'd forgotten to look downward.

And now the foundations were rotten.

"What do you want?"

"Want?" She laughed, and the sound was like breaking glass. "I've been trapped in this hole for ten thousand years, little godling. I want to feel the sun on my skin. I want to taste mortal wine. I want to remember what it feels like to be worshipped."

Wei Chen's fingers found the formation brush in his pocket. If he could just buy enough time to activate the floor's defensive arrays—

"Don't bother." She waved a hand, and the formation arrays he'd been mentally reaching for flared and died. "I've had ten thousand years to study this dungeon's architecture. I know its every weakness. Including its new master."

She moved. He didn't see her cross the distance—she was simply there, standing before him, close enough that he could smell the rot beneath her perfume.

"You're clever," she said, tilting his chin up with one cold finger. "I'll give you that. You took a broken core and made it breathe again. You attracted cultivators, built guardians, created illusions that would have impressed the old court. In a hundred years, you might have been worthy of the title."

Her eyes darkened.

"But a hundred years is a luxury you no longer have."

"Guardian Seven!" Wei Chen shouted.

The celestial soldier lunged forward, spear blazing with golden light. The Crimson Concubine didn't even turn. She simply raised one hand, and Guardian Seven froze mid-strike, its marble flesh cracking, its starlight eyes going dark.

Then it crumbled to dust.

"The dungeon's guardians," she said, turning back to Wei Chen, "draw their power from the core. And the core..." She pressed her palm against his chest, right where the jade token lay. "Is bound to you."

Wei Chen felt it. A coldness seeping through his skin, reaching for the core's presence inside him. The connection that had felt so warm, so reassuring, was now a lifeline being pulled taut.

"Here's what's going to happen," the Crimson Concubine said. "I'm going to take what's mine. The dungeon. The core. The power that was promised to me ten thousand years ago. And you—"

She leaned close, her breath cold against his ear.

"You're going to watch."

The cold intensified. Wei Chen felt something tearing, something fundamental ripping apart inside his chest. The core screamed in his mind, a sound of pure agony, and he screamed with it.

Darkness swallowed his vision.

---

He woke on the floor of the Celestial Audience Hall.

For a moment, he didn't remember. Then the pain hit—a hollow ache in his chest where the core's presence had been. He reached for it, mentally, spiritually, and found only silence.

"Ah, you're awake."

The Crimson Concubine sat on his meditation cushion, legs crossed, hands resting on her knees. She looked different now. More solid. More real. Golden light pulsed around her, and Wei Chen recognized it—that was *his* qi. *His* dungeon's energy.

"The connection is broken," she said, almost sympathetically. "I'm sorry. I know it must hurt. Like losing a limb, or a child. But I needed it more than you did."

Wei Chen tried to stand. His legs wouldn't obey.

"The cultivators will be here soon," she continued. "Your regulars. The ones who've been coming to train, to grow stronger, to test themselves against your illusions. They'll find the dungeon changed. New floors. New guardians. And a new master who's very, very interested in their potential."

"You're going to trap them."

"Trap them?" She laughed. "No, dear boy. I'm going to recruit them. The old Heavenly Court fell because it grew weak, complacent, divided. I won't make the same mistake. I'll build an army. And when I'm ready—"

She stood, and the dungeon trembled around her.

"I'll finish what I started ten thousand years ago."

Wei Chen's hand closed on something cold. The formation brush. He must have held onto it through everything. It was a pathetic weapon, barely more than a tool, but it was all he had.

"One more thing," the Crimson Concubine said, pausing at the door. "Your cultivators. The ones you've been so carefully nurturing. I've already sent word through the dungeon's channels that there's a new floor to explore. A hidden floor. A treasure floor."

She smiled.

"They'll be here within the hour."

She left.

Wei Chen lay on the jade floor, staring up at the golden ceiling, feeling the emptiness where the core had been. The dungeon that had been his, his home, his purpose, was now a prison. And its first prisoners were about to arrive.

Then, in the silence, he heard it.

A whisper.

Faint. Almost inaudible.

But there.

*...still here...*

He pressed his hand to his chest. The core's presence was gone, but something remained. A fragment. A shard.

*...hidden... she doesn't know...*

Wei Chen forced himself to sit up. His body screamed in protest, but he ignored it. He had less than an hour. Less than an hour to figure out how to fight a ten-thousand-year-old monster with nothing but a brush and a dying spark.

He looked at the formation brush in his hand.

And for the first time since he'd bound himself to the core, he started to plan.

title: Chapter 4
content:
CHAPTER 4: The First Trial Begins

The morning of the fourth day arrived with a deep, resonant hum that vibrated through the stone floor and into Wei Chen's bones. He woke from a restless doze in the corner of the audience hall, his back against a pillar, his hand still touching the core fragment he'd embedded in the wall.

The hum was the dungeon. It was *aware*.

"They're coming," he whispered.

The core pulsed in confirmation. *Three cultivators. One at Qi Condensation, second layer. Two at Qi Condensation, first layer. They approach from the eastern ridge.*

Wei Chen scrambled to his feet, his heart hammering. He'd known this moment was coming. He'd prepared for it. But now that it was here, the reality of what he was about to do hit him like a physical blow.

He was about to send actual cultivators into a dungeon. A dungeon that he, a tomb raider with no formal training, had built in three days. If something went wrong—if the illusions failed, if the formations collapsed, if someone got hurt or killed—

*They chose to come,* the core said, its voice calm in his mind. *They seek power. We will give them a trial worthy of the Heavenly Court.*

"Or a tomb worthy of a grave robber," Wei Chen muttered.

He moved quickly, checking his preparations one last time. The audience hall was ready. Guardian One stood in its corner, motionless, its blank face staring at nothing. The illusion formations were active, their patterns invisible to the naked eye but humming with potential energy. The meditation room was prepared, its cushion waiting, its refining formation polished to perfection.

Wei Chen himself stood at the center of the hall, wearing the only decent robe he'd managed to salvage from his pack—a plain gray garment that marked him as nothing more than a mortal servant. That was the image he needed. Weak. Harmless. Beneath notice.

The cultivators would see what he wanted them to see.

He took a deep breath, closed his eyes, and activated the first layer of illusions.

The hall shifted around him. The golden light dimmed to a flickering amber. The jade floor became cracked and worn. Dust seemed to settle on every surface. The murals on the walls faded to indistinct blurs. A musty smell filled the air, the scent of a place long abandoned and forgotten.

The Heavenly Court looked like a ruin.

Wei Chen opened his eyes and surveyed his work. Perfect. Any cultivator who entered would see a recently discovered tomb, not the remnant of a celestial palace. They would think they were exploring an ancient relic, not walking into the heart of a growing dungeon.

And they would underestimate everything.

The three cultivators arrived an hour later.

Wei Chen felt them before he saw them—their qi signatures burning bright in his awareness, a gift from the core that let him sense anyone who entered his domain. The strongest one moved with confidence, his steps sure and steady. The other two followed more cautiously, their energy flickering with uncertainty.

They reached the entrance—a crack in the mountainside that Wei Chen had disguised as a natural cave opening. He'd left a trail of subtle signs for them to find: an old boot print, a scrap of faded cloth, a faint glow from within the cave. Enough to pique their curiosity, not enough to warn them of danger.

"We have visitors," he murmured to the core.

*Let them come.*

The three cultivators stepped through the entrance.

Wei Chen watched them through the dungeon's eyes, seeing them as clearly as if he stood beside them. The leader was a man in his thirties, broad-shouldered and stern-faced, wearing the blue robes of a minor sect. His qi swirled around him like a protective cloak, and his hand rested on the hilt of his sword.

The other two were younger—brothers, from the look of them, with similar features and matching nervous expressions. They carried torches and kept close to their leader, their eyes darting at every shadow.

"It's just an old tomb," the leader said, his voice echoing in the narrow passage. "Probably some minor noble from a forgotten dynasty. Nothing to be afraid of."

The younger brother on the left swallowed hard. "Senior Brother Li, the village elder said people have gone missing near this mountain."

"Village elders say a lot of things," Senior Brother Li replied, dismissing the concern with a wave. "They're superstitious fools who see ghosts in every shadow. We're cultivators. We don't fear the dark."

Wei Chen smiled grimly. *You will.*

The passage led them deeper into the mountain, following a path Wei Chen had carefully designed. It twisted and turned, sometimes narrowing so they had to squeeze through, sometimes opening into small chambers that held nothing but dust and bones. He'd seeded the path with small treasures—a rusted sword here, a broken jade pendant there—to keep them moving, to make them think they were on the right track.

And all the while, the illusion formations were working, subtly influencing their perceptions, making them feel like they were exploring deeper and deeper into an ancient tomb, when in reality they were circling the outer edge of the audience hall.

After an hour of this, Senior Brother Li was getting frustrated.

"This tomb is a maze," he growled, kicking at a pile of bones. "And there's nothing of value anywhere. Just trash and decay."

The younger brother on the right spoke up. "Maybe we should turn back. This doesn't feel right."

"Turn back?" Senior Brother Li laughed. "And let the Zhao Sect claim whatever's at the heart of this place? No. We keep going."

Wei Chen decided it was time for the first test.

He reached out with his will and activated a formation he'd hidden in the floor ahead of them. The stone shifted, and a section of the passage collapsed, blocking their path forward.

Senior Brother Li cursed. "A trap. Stay alert."

They backtracked, looking for another route, and found it easily enough—a side passage that Wei Chen had left open, leading directly into the audience hall.

The moment they stepped through the entrance, Wei Chen felt them cross a threshold. They were in his domain now. The dungeon's power surrounded them on all sides.

The hall appeared before them in its "ruined" state: cracked pillars, faded murals, dusty floor. But even through the illusion, the sheer size of the space made them pause. The ceiling soared into darkness above them. The walls stretched far to either side. And at the far end, on a raised dais, a simple wooden chest sat waiting.

"The main chamber," Senior Brother Li breathed, his eyes fixed on the chest. "And the treasure."

The two younger brothers exchanged glances. "It seems too easy," one said.

"It's a tomb, not a fortress," Senior Brother Li replied, already striding forward. "The traps were at the entrance. The inner chambers are where the dead rest."

Wei Chen held his breath. The chest was the bait. It contained nothing but a few copper coins and a broken talisman—worthless treasures to distract them while the real test began.

Senior Brother Li reached the dais and lifted the lid. His face fell as he saw the contents.

"Trash," he spat. "All of it. This whole tomb is a waste of time."

But as he turned to leave, the illusion shifted.

The hall transformed.

The cracks in the pillars sealed themselves. The dust vanished from the floor. The murals on the walls blazed back to life, their colors vivid and bright. Golden light flooded the space, and the air grew thick with qi.

The Heavenly Court revealed itself.

"What—" Senior Brother Li stumbled back, his hand flying to his sword. "What is this?"

The two younger brothers cried out in alarm, their torches flickering in the sudden brightness.

And then Guardian One stepped forward.

The construct moved with perfect grace, its golden armor gleaming, its featureless face turning toward the cultivators. It raised its spear, and a voice echoed from its chest—not a voice Wei Chen had given it, but something deeper, older, resonating with the power of the core itself.

"WHO DARES ENTER THE HEAVENLY COURT?"

Senior Brother Li's face went pale. "This—this is impossible. The Heavenly Court fell ten thousand years ago!"

"THE COURT FALLS," the guardian intoned, "BUT NEVER FALLS COMPLETELY. THOSE WHO SEEK POWER MUST PROVE THEMSELVES WORTHY."

Wei Chen watched from the shadows, his heart pounding. This was the moment. The guardian was just a construct—strong, but not unbeatable. If the cultivators fought it, they would win. But that wasn't the point.

The point was the test.

The guardian's spear swept toward Senior Brother Li. He blocked it with his sword, the impact sending shockwaves through the hall. The two younger brothers drew their weapons, but before they could join the fight, the illusion formations activated.

The murals on the walls came alive. Figures stepped out of the paintings—celestial soldiers, heavenly maidens, fearsome beasts. They weren't real, but they looked real. They *felt* real. Their presence filled the hall with overwhelming pressure.

"Retreat!" Senior Brother Li shouted, parrying another blow from Guardian One. "Fall back to the entrance!"

But the entrance was gone. Where the passage had been, there was only solid wall.

"The tomb has sealed itself," one of the younger brothers whispered, his voice trembling.

"It's not a tomb," Senior Brother Li said, his eyes wide with realization. "It's a dungeon. A living dungeon."

Wei Chen stepped out of the shadows.

He appeared before them as he had designed himself to appear: a mortal servant, plain and unremarkable, wringing his hands nervously. "Oh, honored cultivators," he said, his voice pitched to sound weak and frightened. "I'm so sorry. I didn't mean to trap you here. I was just cleaning the old palace, and I must have triggered something—"

"You?" Senior Brother Li's eyes narrowed. "A mere mortal did this?"

"I don't know what I did," Wei Chen said, spreading his hands helplessly. "I was just following the master's instructions. He said to keep the halls clean, to make sure nothing disturbed the guardians. But I must have made a mistake."

The younger brothers looked at each other. "The master? What master?"

Wei Chen hesitated, as if afraid to speak. "The master of the palace. The one who sleeps in the inner chambers. He's been asleep for a very long time, but he's starting to wake up. And he doesn't like visitors."

Senior Brother Li's face hardened. "You're lying."

But the fear in his eyes betrayed him. He wasn't sure. And uncertainty was all Wei Chen needed.

"I can show you the way out," Wei Chen offered, his voice gentle, placating. "But only if you agree to leave and never return. The master doesn't want to hurt anyone. He just wants to be left alone."

The silence stretched for an agonizing moment. Then Senior Brother Li nodded slowly.

"Fine. Show us the way out."

Wei Chen smiled inwardly. The first trial was a success. The cultivators had been tested, and they had chosen retreat over greed. They would leave with a story to tell—a story that would spread through the cultivation world, drawing more adventurers to the Heavenly Dungeon.

But as he led them toward the concealed exit, the core pulsed with a warning.

*Another approaches. Different. Stronger.*

Wei Chen's smile faded.

*What kind of stronger?*

The core's answer sent a chill down his spine.

*Foundation Establishment.*

title: The First Adventurers Arrive
content:
Dawn broke over the ruined Heavenly Court like a wound bleeding light.

Wei Chen stood at the entrance of the Celestial Audience Hall, watching the mist crawl across the broken jade pathways. His body ached from three days of nonstop work. His eyes were bloodshot. His robes—the same ones he'd been wearing since he stumbled into this place—smelled like sweat and desperation.

But the dungeon was ready.

Or as ready as it was going to get.

Behind him, the audience hall hummed with barely contained power. Golden light pulsed along the walls in steady rhythms, like a heartbeat. The air was thick with qi, so dense it felt like walking through honey. Guardian One stood at attention near the main pillar, its featureless face scanning the empty hall with programmed vigilance.

And beneath it all, the illusion formations waited. Coiled like sleeping snakes. Ready to strike.

Wei Chen had tested them on himself. He'd nearly broken his own mind in the process, trapped for three hours in a nightmare where the dungeon collapsed on top of him, where the core went dark, where he died alone and forgotten in the ruins of a dead god's palace.

The illusions worked. Maybe too well.

"You're sure about this?" he muttered, pressing a hand to his chest where the core's warmth pulsed beneath his skin. "They're going to come. Real cultivators. With real power. And I'm going to trick them into thinking this place is actually worth something."

The core pulsed. *It is worth something. It is the Heavenly Court.*

"Was. Past tense. Right now it's a glorified tomb with good lighting."

A pulse, almost amused. *Then we will make it become again.*

Wei Chen shook his head but didn't argue. He'd learned in the past three days that arguing with the core was like arguing with a mountain. It would listen, but it would not change.

He turned back to the hall one more time, running through his mental checklist. The meditation room was complete. The main hall was functional. The illusion formations were active. Guardian One was positioned to guide visitors deeper into the dungeon. The trial—a simple test of qi control—was set to begin at the entrance.

It wasn't much. But it was enough for a first floor.

"Here goes nothing," Wei Chen said, and stepped out onto the jade pathway to wait.

---

They came at noon.

Wei Chen felt them before he saw them—a ripple in the ambient qi, like stones thrown into a still pond. Three presences, all of them Qi Condensation stage. One at the third level, two at the second. Weak by the standards of the old Heavenly Court. Perfect for his purposes.

He straightened his robes and forced a calm expression onto his face as three figures emerged from the mist.

The leader was a young man in blue silk robes, his hair bound in a jade crown. He carried a sword at his hip—not a spiritual weapon, but good steel, well-maintained. His face was sharp, arrogant, the kind of face that had never been told "no" by anyone below his station.

Behind him walked two more cultivators. One was a woman in practical leather armor, her eyes scanning the ruins with professional wariness. The other was a heavyset man carrying a large axe, his expression bored.

The blue-robed young man stopped when he saw Wei Chen, his eyes narrowing.

"You." The word was sharp, demanding. "Who are you? What is this place?"

Wei Chen bowed, keeping his movements measured and calm. "I am the caretaker of this cultivation ground. You have discovered the entrance to the Jade Emperor's training halls."

All three cultivators stiffened.

The young man's hand went to his sword. "The Jade Emperor? That's a fairy tale. A children's story."

"Is it?" Wei Chen smiled, letting a hint of condescension creep into his voice. "Then why are you here?"

The young man's face reddened. He opened his mouth to retort, but the woman stepped forward, her hand on his arm.

"Brother Liu. Calm yourself." She turned to Wei Chen, her eyes measuring. "We followed rumors. Whispers in the mortal world about a place of power emerging in these mountains. We didn't expect..." She gestured at the ruins around them. "This."

"Few do," Wei Chen said. "Heaven has fallen. But its foundations remain. I have been tasked with rebuilding what was lost. And I am looking for cultivators worthy of its legacy."

The heavyset man snorted. "Worthy? You're Qi Condensation at best. Probably first level. What makes you qualified to judge anyone?"

Wei Chen's smile didn't waver. "I am not the judge. The dungeon is." He stepped aside, gesturing toward the entrance of the Celestial Audience Hall. "If you wish to prove yourselves, enter. Survive the trial. Claim the rewards that await within."

Brother Liu's eyes glittered with greed. "Rewards? What kind of rewards?"

"Spiritual treasures. Cultivation techniques. The blessings of the old Heavenly Court." Wei Chen spread his hands. "All of it awaits those who prove worthy."

The three cultivators exchanged glances. Wei Chen could see the calculations running through their minds—the greed warring with caution, the desire for power clashing with the fear of the unknown.

Greed won.

"Fine," Brother Liu said, pushing past Wei Chen. "But if this is a trap, caretaker, I will personally ensure your death is slow."

Wei Chen bowed again. "I would expect nothing less."

The three cultivators stepped through the entrance.

As soon as they crossed the threshold, the illusion formations activated. The air shimmered. The golden light intensified. And the Celestial Audience Hall transformed before their eyes.

Where there had been ruins and broken pillars, now stood a grand hall of impossible beauty. Golden pillars stretched toward a sky that shouldn't exist, their surfaces carved with dragons and phoenixes that seemed to move in the corner of the eye. The jade floor gleamed with inner light, reflecting the faces of the cultivators as they walked. Mist curled around their ankles, carrying the scent of celestial flowers.

The woman gasped. The heavyset man's axe lowered slightly, his eyes wide.

Brother Liu's arrogance cracked, just a little.

"This is..." he whispered.

"The first floor," Wei Chen said from behind them, his voice carrying through the hall with an echo that hadn't been there before. "The Celestial Audience Hall. Where cultivators once came to seek the Jade Emperor's blessing."

He watched them take it all in, their awe spreading across their faces like dawn across a dark sky. For a moment, he almost felt guilty. The illusion was beautiful, yes. But it was a lie. A pretty mask covering a broken face.

Then Brother Liu turned to him, and the arrogance returned. "What must we do? What is the trial?"

Wei Chen pointed to the center of the hall, where a golden formation now glowed on the floor. "Stand in the formation. Channel your qi into it. The dungeon will judge your control."

"That's it?" The heavyset man laughed. "Anyone can channel qi."

"Anyone can *push* qi," Wei Chen corrected. "Controlling it is another matter entirely. The formation will test the purity and precision of your energy. It will not accept brute force."

Brother Liu stepped into the formation without hesitation. The woman followed, her movements more cautious. The heavyset man shrugged and joined them.
