The air was thick with the stench of decay, and the sky—what should have been a perfect illusion of the heavens—was a churning mass of red and black clouds.

"It's getting stronger," the guardian said, her voice calm. "The entity is spreading its influence."

"Where are the cultivators?"

"This way."

She led him through the corrupted floor, past twisted formations and broken bridges. The ichor seemed to reach for them as they passed, but the guardian's presence kept it at bay, her golden light burning through the darkness.

They found the first cultivator near the center of the floor.

It was the Qi Condensation disciple from the Azure Cloud Sect. He was huddled behind a broken pillar, his sword drawn, his face pale with terror. When he saw Wei Chen, his eyes widened.

"Dungeon Master!"

"Stay calm," Wei Chen said, helping him to his feet. "I'm getting you out of here."

"The others—"

"I know. Where are they?"

The disciple pointed a trembling hand toward a massive structure in the distance. It looked like a palace, but like everything else on this floor, it was twisted and corrupted.

"They went inside. They said there was a treasure. But it was a trap. It was all a trap."

Wei Chen's blood ran cold. "A treasure?"

"Yes. A jade seal. They said it was the seal of a celestial general. But when my senior touched it, the darkness came."

A jade seal.

Wei Chen had not placed any jade seal on the seventeenth floor.

"Core," he said, but there was no response. The seal was blocking his connection.

He was on his own.

"Guardian, take this cultivator back to the gate. I'll find the others."

"Master—"

"That's an order."

The guardian hesitated, then nodded. She took the disciple's arm and led him away, her golden light fading into the distance.

Wei Chen turned toward the corrupted palace.

"Alright," he said, drawing his own sword

title: The First Tribulation Walks In
content:
The first cultivator arrived at dawn.

Wei Chen felt him before he saw him—a ripple in the dungeon's awareness, like a stone dropped into still water. He was meditating in the control room when the sensation hit, jolting him from his trance. The core pulsed a warning through their connection, and images flooded his mind: a man approaching from the east, walking along the crumbling path that had once been the Celestial processional way.

*Cultivator,* the core whispered. *Qi Condensation, sixth layer. Carrying a sword. Coming quickly.*

"He's early," Wei Chen muttered, scrambling to his feet. "I thought they wouldn't come until noon."

The core had no answer for that. It simply pulsed again, showing him the man's progress—past the fallen pillars, through the broken gate, up the stairs that Wei Chen had spent half a day repairing. In fifteen minutes, he would reach the entrance.

Wei Chen's heart hammered against his ribs. He wasn't ready. The illusions were barely tested. Guardian One stood in the corner like a golden statue with no idea what to do if someone actually attacked. And the meditation room—the only real feature of the dungeon—was a single cushion on a floor.

"Alright," he said, forcing calm into his voice. "Alright. We knew this was coming. What do I do?"

The core pulsed. *You are the dungeon master. You control everything within these walls. Act like it.*

"Act like it. Right. Easy."

He closed his eyes and reached out with his new senses. The dungeon spread before him like a map drawn in light and qi. He could feel every stone, every formation, every wisp of spiritual energy that drifted through the halls. The main entrance was a gaping wound in the fabric of the dungeon—a door that had been torn off its hinges centuries ago and never replaced. Beyond it, the path wound through a short corridor and into the Celestial Audience Hall.

The first floor was simple. Too simple. A single room, a single guardian, a single trap.

But the trap was good.

Wei Chen had spent the last three days learning the illusion technique the core had shown him. It was called the Maze of Reflections, and it was beautiful in its cruelty. The formation created a pocket dimension that mirrored the real room but twisted it—reflections that moved independently, corridors that led nowhere, paths that doubled back on themselves. A cultivator trapped inside would see only endless copies of the same space, their own reflection staring back at them from a hundred angles.

The kicker? It fed on their qi. Every moment they spent trying to break free, the formation drained them a little more. If they were weak enough, it could strip them down to nothing but a mortal shell.

Wei Chen had tested it on himself. It worked. It scared him.

He took a deep breath and began to activate the formation.

The qi in the dungeon stirred. Golden light pulsed along the walls, brighter and faster than before. The air grew thick, heavy with spiritual pressure. In the audience hall, the murals seemed to come alive—the gods in the paintings shifted, their eyes tracking something unseen. The jade floor rippled like water disturbed by a breeze.

And then the formation snapped into place.

Wei Chen felt it click, felt the world fold in on itself as the Maze of Reflections settled over the audience hall like a second skin. To anyone standing in the room, nothing would look different. But beneath the surface, reality had become a lie.

"Okay," he whispered. "Okay. It's active. Now I just need to—"

The core pulsed. *He's at the entrance.*

Wei Chen opened his eyes and looked at the viewing mirror he'd created—a simple bronze disk that showed him whatever part of the dungeon he wished to see. In its surface, a man stepped through the broken door.

He was young, maybe twenty-five, with the lean build of a fighter. He wore blue robes of good quality, and a sword hung at his waist—not a cheap iron blade, either. Wei Chen could see the faint shimmer of spiritual steel along its edge. The man's face was sharp, arrogant, his eyes scanning the corridor with undisguised contempt.

"The Heavenly Court," he muttered, his voice carrying through the dungeon's acoustics. "What a joke. This place is a ruin."

He walked forward without hesitation, his footsteps echoing on the jade floor. When he reached the entrance to the audience hall, he stopped.

Wei Chen held his breath.

The man studied the room. His eyes passed over Guardian One without interest—the construct was motionless, its blank face turned toward the wall. He looked at the pillars, the murals, the misty ceiling. He sniffed the air, and his expression shifted from contempt to surprise.

"The qi here is..." He trailed off, taking a deeper breath. "Pure. Very pure. Maybe this place isn't completely worthless."

He stepped into the room.

The Maze of Reflections activated instantly.

Wei Chen saw it happen through the viewing mirror—saw the man's image split, duplicate, multiply. Suddenly there were a hundred identical cultivators standing in a hundred identical rooms, all of them looking around in confusion. The real one—Wei Chen tracked him through the core's senses—stopped mid-stride, his hand going to his sword.

"What is this?" His voice echoed strangely, bouncing off walls that weren't there. "An illusion formation? In this dump?"

He drew his sword. It gleamed with spiritual light, and Wei Chen felt a spike of worry. This wasn't some random bandit with a rusty blade. This was a real cultivator, with real training, facing his first real test.

The man slashed at the air in front of him. The illusion rippled but held. He slashed again, harder, and Wei Chen felt the formation groan under the strain.

*He's strong,* the core pulsed. *Stronger than expected. His technique is crude, but his qi is dense.*

"How long can the formation hold?"

*Against this cultivator? Twenty minutes. Maybe less.*

Wei Chen cursed. Twenty minutes wasn't enough. He needed something else, something that could end this faster. He looked at Guardian One, still motionless in the corner, and an idea formed.

"What if I send in the guardian?"

*The guardian is incomplete. It cannot fight.*

"It doesn't need to fight. It just needs to scare him."

There was a pause. Then the core pulsed with something that felt almost like amusement. *That might work. But be careful. If he attacks the guardian, it will shatter.*

"Then I'd better make it convincing."

Wei Chen reached out with his mind, taking control of Guardian One. The construct felt strange—hollow, like a puppet with no strings. He made it turn. Made it walk. Made it stop at the edge of the illusion, where the man could see it but couldn't reach it.

Then he opened its mouth and spoke.

"WHO DARES ENTER THE CELESTIAL AUDIENCE HALL WITHOUT PERMISSION?"

The voice boomed through the room, amplified by the dungeon's formations. Wei Chen had tried to make it sound like a god's voice—deep, commanding, filled with ancient power. It came out more like a shouting merchant, but he hoped it was enough.

The cultivator froze. His sword dropped slightly as he stared at the golden construct.

"This is... a guardian?" He sounded uncertain for the first time. "It's still functional?"

"YOU STAND BEFORE THE THRONE OF THE JADE EMPEROR," Wei Chen continued, trying to sound more impressive. "STATE YOUR NAME AND PURPOSE, OR BE STRUCK DOWN."

The man's arrogance returned. He straightened, sheathed his sword, and gave a shallow bow—more mockery than respect.

"I am Lin Feng, disciple of the Crimson Cloud Sect. I've come to explore this ruin and claim whatever treasures remain."

*Treasures,* Wei Chen thought bitterly. *All I have is a cushion and a half-finished illusion.*

"THERE ARE NO TREASURES HERE FOR THE UNWORTHY," he said. "LEAVE NOW, AND I WILL SPARE YOUR LIFE."

Lin Feng laughed. "Spare my life? You're a broken construct in a broken temple. I'd like to see you try."

He drew his sword again and charged.

Wei Chen panicked. He made Guardian One raise its spear, but the movement was too slow, too clumsy. Lin Feng dodged easily, his blade flashing toward the construct's chest—

And stopped.

The Maze of Reflections shifted, sending Lin Feng stumbling into a corner of the room that hadn't existed a moment before. He hit the wall hard, gasping as the formation drained a thread of his qi.

Wei Chen let out a shaky breath. "That was close."

*He's learning,* the core warned. *The formation won't fool him for much longer.*

It was right. Lin Feng was already getting to his feet, his eyes scanning the room with renewed focus. He was tracing the flow of qi, looking for the formation's anchor points. In another few minutes, he would find them.

Wei Chen needed a new plan.

He looked around the control room, searching for anything he could use. The core sat in its pedestal, pulsing with soft light. The viewing mirror showed Lin Feng's progress. A few scraps of paper lay on the floor—notes he'd taken during his practice, diagrams of formations he'd copied from the core's memories.

One of the diagrams caught his eye.

It was a simple binding formation, used by the old Heavenly Court to restrain prisoners. It required the user to channel qi through a specific pattern while maintaining eye contact with the target. Wei Chen had dismissed it earlier as too difficult to master in three days.

But now, watching Lin Feng methodically dismantle his illusion, he wondered if he had a choice.

"Core," he said. "Can you show me that binding formation again? The one from the archives."

The core pulsed. A stream of information flowed into his mind—the precise sequence of hand gestures, the flow of qi through his meridians, the mental focus required to lock the target in place. It was complex. It was delicate. If he made a single mistake, the backlash could kill him.

But it was his only shot.

"Alright," he said, stepping toward the door. "I'm going in."

*That is unwise. You are still weak. If he attacks you directly—*

"I know. But if I don't stop him, he'll break the formation and walk out with everything I've built. Then he'll tell his friends, and they'll come, and it'll all be over." Wei Chen took a deep breath. "I didn't bind myself to a Heavenly Dungeon Core just to die in a basement. I'm going out there."

The core pulsed, and for a moment, Wei Chen felt something like pride through their connection. *You have the arrogance of a true Jade Emperor.*

"I'll take that as a compliment."

He walked out of the control room and into the audience hall.

Lin Feng saw him immediately. The cultivator's eyes widened as Wei Chen stepped through the illusion—which parted for him like a curtain—and stood before the golden pillars. For a long moment, they just stared at each other.

Then Lin Feng laughed.

"You? You're the one controlling this place?" He shook his head, his sword lowering slightly. "I thought I was dealing with a real guardian. Instead I get a mortal with a trick formation."

"Leave," Wei Chen said. His voice was steadier than he felt. "Leave now, and I won't hurt you."

"Hurt me?" Lin Feng's grin was vicious. "I'm going to take that formation from your corpse and sell it for a fortune."

He lunged.

title: The First Invaders Arrive
content:
The morning of the fourth day arrived with a sound Wei Chen had been dreading—footsteps, echoing through the outer tunnels.

He'd been meditating in the audience hall, his consciousness spread thin across the dungeon's sparse network of qi veins. The moment he sensed the intruders, his eyes snapped open.

*Three of them,* the core pulsed. *Qi Condensation, fifth layer. Armed.*

"Three?" Wei Chen scrambled to his feet, his heart hammering. "I was expecting one, maybe two. And they're fifth layer? That's almost Foundation Establishment."

*They are tomb raiders. Like you were.*

"Was," Wei Chen corrected, a bitter taste in his mouth. "I'm not a tomb raider anymore. I'm a dungeon master." He paused. "Wait, how do you know they're tomb raiders?"

*I remember the feel of their kind. Desperation. Greed. The stench of grave dirt.*

Wei Chen shuddered. The core's words were too accurate for comfort. He'd smelled that same stench on himself for years, never quite able to wash it out of his clothes, his skin, his soul.

"Alright. Show me where they are."

The core obliged. A map materialized in Wei Chen's mind—crude, incomplete, but enough. The three intruders were making their way through the outer tunnels, moving with the practiced caution of men who'd spent their lives navigating deadly traps. They carried torches that cast dancing shadows on the walls, and their hands never strayed far from their weapons.

"Can you tell what they're looking for?"

*They seek treasure. Power. The same things all mortals seek when they enter the domains of gods.*

"Great. So they're going to be disappointed when they find a half-finished meditation room and a guardian with no face."

*Perhaps. Or perhaps they will find something worse.*

The core's tone was strange—almost amused. Wei Chen didn't like it.

"What do you mean?"

*You will see. The illusions are ready. The formations are set. But you must guide them. Lead them where you want them to go.*

"I've never done this before. What if I mess up?"

*Then we die.*

Wei Chen swallowed hard. "No pressure."

He took a deep breath and extended his consciousness into the dungeon's qi network. It was like stretching a muscle he'd never used before—painful, awkward, but possible. He could feel the outer tunnels now, could sense the rough stone walls and the faint traces of residual energy left by ancient rituals.

And he could feel the three men.

They were close. Too close.

"Guardian One," he whispered. The golden construct in the corner stirred, its blank face turning toward him. "Stay hidden. Wait for my signal."

The construct nodded once and dissolved into light, merging with the walls.

Wei Chen retreated to the meditation room, leaving the audience hall empty and silent. He positioned himself behind the illusionary formation, his hands pressed against the floor, channeling qi into the arrays he'd spent the last three days perfecting.

The first man entered the audience hall.

He was tall and wiry, with a scar running from his temple to his jaw. His eyes were sharp, darting across the room, assessing every corner, every shadow. He carried a dao sword in one hand and a talisman in the other—a detection charm, Wei Chen realized with a chill.

*He's prepared. He knows this place is dangerous.*

The second man followed close behind, broader and slower, carrying a heavy pack that clanked with tools. The third brought up the rear, a bow already strung and an arrow nocked.

"Empty," the first man said, his voice echoing in the vast hall. "Just like the reports said."

"Reports?" Wei Chen muttered under his breath. "What reports?"

The second man grunted. "Doesn't look empty to me. Look at those walls. That's not natural stone. That's jade."

"Jade's worthless if we can't carry it out," the first man said. "We're here for the core. Nothing else."

*They know about me,* the core pulsed, its voice tight with something that might have been fear. *Someone sent them.*

Wei Chen's blood ran cold. "Who?"

*I do not know. But they are not ordinary tomb raiders. They have information. Purpose.*

The first man walked to the center of the hall, his detection charm glowing faintly. He held it up, turning in a slow circle, his eyes narrowing as the charm's light flickered.

"There's something here," he said. "A formation. Hidden."

"You can break it?" the second man asked.

"Give me time."

Wei Chen's hands trembled. The illusionary formation was his only defense. If they broke through, he'd have nothing—no walls, no weapons, no army of celestial soldiers. Just himself and a half-formed dungeon.

*You must act,* the core urged. *Now.*

"I don't know how—"

*You do. Trust yourself.*

Wei Chen closed his eyes. He let his consciousness flow into the formation, feeling its strands of qi like threads of silk. He touched them, gently, and began to weave.

The hall shifted.

The first man froze, his detection charm flaring bright. "Something's happening. Stay alert."

The walls began to ripple, the golden light pulsing in waves. The murals came alive—celestial generals marching across the stone, their eyes burning with divine fire. The mist that had filled the ceiling descended, thick and cold, swallowing the pillars one by one.

"It's an illusion," the first man said, but his voice wavered. "Hold your ground."

Wei Chen pushed harder. The mist coalesced into shapes—armored soldiers, their faces twisted in fury. They raised their weapons and charged.

The third man loosed an arrow. It passed straight through the nearest soldier, clattering against the far wall.

"Illusion," he confirmed, relief in his voice. "Can't hurt us."

"Can't it?" Wei Chen whispered.

He twisted the formation.

The soldiers dissolved, but the mist remained. And now, from within the mist, came sounds—the clash of weapons, the screams of dying men, the roar of beasts. The sounds grew louder, closer, until they seemed to come from everywhere at once.

The three men pressed together, backs against each other, weapons raised.

"Steady," the first man said. "It's just tricks. Whoever's running this place is trying to scare us."

"He's doing a good job," the second man muttered.

Wei Chen grinned. He wasn't trying to scare them. He was buying time.

But time for what?

The answer came as a pulse from the core—urgent, desperate. *They are not alone. More are coming. From the outside.*

"What? How many?"

*I cannot tell. But they are stronger. Much stronger.*

Wei Chen's grin vanished. He'd been so focused on the three intruders that he hadn't extended his senses beyond the dungeon walls. Now he did, and what he found made his stomach drop.

A squad of cultivators was approaching the entrance. A dozen at least, all at the peak of Qi Condensation. And leading them was a man in white robes, his aura burning like a small sun.

Foundation Establishment.

"Who the hell is that?" Wei Chen whispered.

The core was silent for a long moment. Then it showed him an image—a memory, old and bitter. A man in white robes, standing in the ruins of the Heavenly Court, laughing as celestial soldiers fell around him.

*The Pure Void Sect,* the core said, its voice trembling with ancient rage. *They were the first to betray the Jade Emperor. They are the reason the Heavenly Court fell.*

Wei Chen's blood ran cold. "They're looking for you."

*Yes. And now they have found me.*

The three tomb raiders in the audience hall had stopped moving. They were listening now, too—their heads cocked, their eyes wide. They'd heard something. The sound of the approaching squad, maybe, or the core's angry pulse.

"What's happening?" the second man asked.

The first man's face was pale. "We're not alone. There's someone else in this dungeon. Someone powerful."

*He's right,* the core said. *And he's about to become very dead.*

Before Wei Chen could ask what that meant, the entrance to the audience hall exploded inward.

Stone shattered. Dust billowed. And through the breach stepped the man in white robes, his sword drawn, his eyes blazing with cold light.

"I am Elder Hao of the Pure Void Sect," he announced, his voice carrying the weight of absolute authority. "By order of the sect master, I claim this dungeon in the name of the Heavenly Restoration."

The three tomb raiders turned to face him, their weapons raised. But Wei Chen could see their hands shaking. They knew, as he knew, that they were outmatched.

"You have no authority here," the first man said, his voice steady despite his trembling hands. "We found it first."

Elder Hao smiled. It was not a pleasant smile.

"Then you will die first."

He moved.

Wei Chen didn't see the attack. One moment, Elder Hao was standing at the entrance. The next, the first tomb raider was on the ground, his chest pierced by a blade of pure qi. The other two didn't even have time to scream before they joined him.

Three bodies. Three seconds.

Elder Hao flicked the blood from his sword and looked around the audience hall, his gaze passing over the illusionary formation as if it didn't exist.

"Interesting," he said. "A fledgling dungeon. Barely formed. And yet the core's energy is unmistakable." He paused, tilting his head. "Show yourself, dungeon master. I know you're here."

Wei Chen didn't move. He barely breathed.

"I can sense your qi," Elder Hao continued, his voice soft and dangerous. "You're weak. Unrefined. A mortal playing with powers you don't understand." He laughed. "Did you think you could hide the Heavenly Court's core? Did you think no one would notice?"

The core pulsed, hot and angry. *He is lying. He does not know where you are. He is trying to draw you out.*

"I know," Wei Chen whispered.

But Elder Hao's next words made his blood freeze.

"The sect master has a message for you, dungeon master." The elder's smile widened. "The Jade Emperor's throne will not be rebuilt by a grave robber. Surrender the core, and we will let you live."

Silence.

Wei Chen's mind raced. How did they know? How did they know who he was, what he had? He'd only been the dungeon master for four days.

The core pulsed again, and this time, Wei Chen felt something new—a connection, stretching outward from the dungeon, thin and fragile. A thread of fate, linking him to something beyond.

*They have a diviner,* the core said. *Someone who can see the threads of destiny. They knew you would find me before you did.*

Wei Chen's hands clenched into fists. "So they've been watching me this whole time?"

*Yes. And now they are here to take what is mine.*

Elder Hao was still waiting, his sword ready, his aura blazing. Behind him, the squad of cultivators filed into the hall, their formation tight, their weapons drawn.

Wei Chen had three choices.

He could surrender. Give up the core, let the Pure Void Sect take it, and hope they kept their promise to let him live. But he knew, deep in his gut, that they wouldn't. He knew too much.

He could fight.

title: The First Adventurers Arrive
content:
The morning sun had not yet breached the mountains when Wei Chen felt them coming.

He was in the meditation room, cross-legged on the cushion, his consciousness spread through the dungeon like spider silk. The core had taught him this trick on the second day—a way to sense disturbances in the qi field that surrounded the Heavenly Dungeon. At first, the sensation had been vague, like trying to hear a whisper through a hurricane. But now, after three days of constant practice, he could feel the individual vibrations of approaching life.

There were five of them. Moving fast. Purposefully.

Wei Chen's eyes snapped open. "They're early."

He scrambled to his feet, his robes rustling in the silence. The dungeon's first floor gleamed around him—the golden pillars now etched with formation arrays that hummed with barely contained power, the jade floor polished to a mirror sheen, the misty ceiling hiding secrets he hadn't yet unlocked. Guardian One stood at attention near the entrance, its featureless face turned toward the door.

Wei Chen had made improvements. The construct's armor now had actual definition—shoulder plates, a breastplate etched with cloud patterns, greaves that caught the light. Its spear had gained a blade of condensed spiritual energy, sharp enough to cut through mortal steel. But its face remained blank. That would have to wait.

"You," Wei Chen said, pointing at the guardian. "Stand here. Look imposing. If they attack, don't kill them. Just... discourage."

The guardian didn't respond. It couldn't. But it shifted its stance slightly, planting its feet wider, angling its spear forward.

Wei Chen took a deep breath. Then he walked to the entrance of the audience hall and waited.

The five cultivators arrived ten minutes later.

They came through the main doors—the massive bronze portals that had once welcomed celestial officials to the Jade Emperor's court. Now they hung crooked on their hinges, covered in centuries of grime and neglect. But the cultivators didn't seem to care. They pushed through without hesitation, their weapons drawn, their eyes scanning for threats.

Wei Chen studied them as they entered. The leader was a man in his forties, built like a bear, with a scar running from his temple to his jaw. He wore the robes of a minor sect—blue and white, with a crane embroidered on the chest. His cultivation was solid. Qi Condensation, sixth level. Strong enough to cause problems, weak enough to be manageable.

Behind him came a woman with a bow, her eyes constantly moving, her fingers never far from the string. Third level Qi Condensation. A scout. Then two younger men, brothers by the look of them, both carrying swords and wearing matching scuffed leather armor. Second level. Greenhorns.

And bringing up the rear...

Wei Chen's breath caught.

A girl. No more than sixteen. She wore simple gray robes, the kind given to outer disciples in the smallest sects. Her cultivation was barely perceptible—Qi Condensation, first level, and shaky at that. She carried no weapon. Her eyes were downcast, her shoulders hunched, as if she expected to be struck at any moment.

*What is she doing here?* Wei Chen thought. *This isn't a training ground. This is a tomb.*

The scarred leader spotted him first. "You," he said, his voice rough. "Are you the caretaker?"

Wei Chen raised an eyebrow. "Caretaker?"

"The old man who sent the message. Said this place had opened up. Said there were treasures inside."

Wei Chen's mind raced. Someone had spread word about the dungeon. That was both good and bad. Good, because it meant cultivators would come. Bad, because he had no control over who knew or what they expected.

"I'm the... guide," Wei Chen said carefully. "This dungeon is new. Untested. I wouldn't recommend bringing—"

"Save your breath," the leader cut him off. "We know the risks. We've been doing this for years. Tombs, ruins, ancient temples. This is just another one."

"It's not."

"What?"

"This isn't just another ruin," Wei Chen said, stepping forward. He let his voice drop, let a hint of the dungeon's power seep into his words. "This is the Heavenly Court. The seat of the Jade Emperor. The gods themselves once walked these halls."

The leader snorted. "Empty gods. Dead gods. Their treasures are ours for the taking."

"Or their traps are yours for the springing."

The leader's hand went to his sword. "Are you threatening us, guide?"

"I'm warning you." Wei Chen met his eyes. "The first floor is safe. For now. But if you push deeper, if you take what isn't offered, the dungeon will respond. And it won't be gentle."

The woman with the bow shifted, her fingers brushing her string. "Brother, maybe we should—"

"No." The leader's voice was granite. "We came here for treasure. We're leaving with treasure. Guide, step aside."

Wei Chen didn't move.

For a long moment, the two of them stared at each other. The dungeon hummed around them, the golden light pulsing in time with Wei Chen's heartbeat. Guardian One stood motionless, its blank face somehow menacing.

Then the leader laughed. "Fine. Have it your way. We'll play by the dungeon's rules. For now." He gestured at his group. "Fan out. Check every corner. Touch nothing until I say so."

The two brothers moved immediately, spreading to the sides of the hall. The woman with the bow climbed a collapsed pillar, gaining height. The girl in gray stayed where she was, her eyes still downcast.

Wei Chen watched them work. They were efficient, he had to give them that. They checked the walls for hidden compartments, tested the floor for pressure plates, examined the pillars for formation arrays. They knew what they were doing.

But they didn't know about the illusions.

Wei Chen had placed them carefully. The first was at the entrance—a subtle distortion that made the hall seem larger than it was, that hid the true dimensions of the space. The second was in the center of the room, a shimmer in the air that made the jade floor look solid when it was actually a thin layer over a pit. The third was the most dangerous: a false treasure chest, gleaming with gold and jewels, that would disappear the moment someone tried to open it.

The illusions weren't perfect. They were the best he could manage in three days, but they had gaps, weaknesses. A keen-eyed cultivator might spot them.

The brothers didn't.

They walked straight into the center of the room, their boots thudding on the jade floor. The shimmer in the air flickered, and for a moment, the pit beneath them was visible—a dark hole that dropped into nothing.

Then the illusion reasserted itself, and they kept walking, oblivious.

Wei Chen let out a breath he hadn't known he was holding.

"Nothing here," one of the brothers called out. "Just empty space."

"Keep looking," the leader growled.

The woman with the bow jumped down from her pillar. "There's a door at the back. Leads deeper in."

"We're not going deeper," the leader said. "Not yet. We secure the first floor, then we establish a base camp."

The girl in gray finally looked up. Her eyes met Wei Chen's, and he saw something there that made his chest tighten. Fear. Not the fear of the dungeon, or the fear of the unknown. The fear of someone who had been hurt before. Who expected to be hurt again.

*Who are you?* Wei Chen wondered. *What are you doing with these people?*

But before he could say anything, the leader turned to him. "Guide. You said the first floor was safe. Show us where the real treasures are."

Wei Chen's jaw tightened. He had prepared for this. Sort of. He had placed a few genuine treasures on the first floor—small things, baubles that would satisfy greedy cultivators without giving away the dungeon's true power. A jade pendant that stored a small amount of qi. A bronze bell that chimed with spiritual energy. A scroll containing a basic cultivation technique.

"Follow me," he said.

He led them to a side chamber, one of the smaller rooms off the main hall. In the center, on a stone pedestal, sat the jade pendant. It glowed with a soft, green light, pulsing gently.

The two brothers rushed forward.

"Stop," Wei Chen said.

They froze.

"That pendant is protected by a formation. If you grab it without knowing the sequence, it will burn your hand off."

The leader narrowed his eyes. "You know the sequence?"

"I do."

"Show me."

Wei Chen walked to the pedestal. He had, in fact, placed a simple formation around the pendant—one that would shock anyone who touched it without first channeling qi in a specific pattern. He demonstrated the pattern now, his fingers tracing the air, his qi flowing through the dungeon's veins.

The pendant lifted from the pedestal and floated into his hand.

"There," he said, holding it out. "One genuine treasure. Qi storage, about a week's worth of cultivation. Worth a few spirit stones, maybe more to the right buyer."

The leader took it, turning it over in his hands. "Not bad. But we're going to need more than this."

"This is all the first floor has to offer."

"I don't believe you."

Wei Chen met his gaze. "Believe what you want. But I'm telling you the truth. This dungeon is new. It hasn't had time to generate deeper treasures. The second floor might have more, but I wouldn't recommend going there without proper preparation."

"I'll decide what's proper." The leader pocketed the pendant. "We're staying the night. We'll explore the second floor tomorrow."

"That's a mistake."

"No one asked you."

The leader turned and walked away, his group following. The girl in gray lingered for a moment, her eyes meeting Wei Chen's again. She opened her mouth, as if to say something, then closed it and hurried after the others.

Wei Chen watched them go. His hands were shaking.

*I can't let them go to the second floor,* he thought. *It's not ready. It's not safe.*

But he had no way to stop them. Not without revealing his true nature. Not without using the dungeon's power in a way that would attract attention he couldn't afford.

The core pulsed in his mind. *They will die.*

"I know."

*The second floor is incomplete. The formations are unstable. The guardians have not been placed.*

"I know."

*We must do something.*

Wei Chen closed his eyes. He felt the dungeon around him, felt its presence settling in his bones. He was not alone. The dungeon was with him. The old Heavenly Court, broken and battered, had placed its trust in him.

And he had to protect it.

"Alright," he said, opening his eyes. "I'll go with them. Keep them out of trouble."

*That is not wise. You are not strong enough to—*

"I know that too." Wei Chen started walking toward the back door, toward the second floor. "But I'm all they've got."

He reached the door and paused. Behind him, in the main hall, Guardian One stood motionless, its blank face watching him go.

Wei Chen took a deep breath.

Then he pushed open the door and stepped into the darkness.

The second floor was not ready. It was a chaotic mess of half-formed structures, unstable formations, and lurking dangers that even Wei Chen didn't fully understand. The cultivators would find no treasure here. Only death.

And somewhere in the depths, something ancient

title: The First Trial Begins
content:
The morning sun cast pale fingers of light through the broken windows of the Celestial Audience Hall. Wei Chen stood at the entrance, his newly crafted robes—simple gray cloth with golden thread at the cuffs—billowing in a breeze that shouldn't exist inside a dungeon. He'd spent the last hour pacing, checking every formation, every illusion, every corner of his pathetic first floor.

Guardian One stood motionless in the center of the hall, spear held at attention, featureless face turned toward the entrance. The illusion formations hummed beneath the floor, ready to activate at his command. The meditation room sat empty, its cushion pristine, its inscriptions glowing with soft amber light.

It wasn't much. But it was all he had.

"Any sign of them?" he asked the core.

*Three cultivators. Approaching from the east. One Nascent Soul. Two Core Formation.*

Wei Chen's blood ran cold. "Nascent Soul? I thought we'd get Qi Condensation at best!"

*The Heavenly Dungeon's presence is... noticeable. Strong cultivators sense opportunity.*

"Opportunity or death sentence?" Wei Chen muttered. He'd never fought a Nascent Soul cultivator. He'd never fought anyone, really—his tomb raiding career had relied on stealth, traps he'd set weeks in advance, and running away. Direct confrontation was not in his skillset.

The core pulsed with what might have been amusement. *You have me.*

"That's supposed to be comforting?"

*It is not.*

Wei Chen cursed under his breath and moved to the shadows beside the entrance. He'd prepared a spot—a small alcove hidden by an illusion formation, where he could observe without being seen. The core had taught him the technique, and he'd practiced until his head throbbed. Now he pressed his back against the cold jade wall and waited.

They came through the entrance like gods descending to judge the unworthy.

The leader was a woman in her forties, dressed in robes of deep purple silk that shimmered with embedded formation arrays. Her hair was pulled back in a severe bun, held in place by a jade hairpin that radiated killing intent. Her eyes swept the hall with cold assessment, taking in every detail in the space of a breath.

Behind her came two men. One was young, barely twenty, with arrogant eyes and a sword at his hip that practically hummed with spiritual energy. The other was older, weathered, his face a mask of professional disinterest. Both wore the uniforms of a sect Wei Chen didn't recognize—black robes with silver trim, a stylized mountain peak embroidered over their hearts.

"So this is it," the young man said, his voice echoing in the empty hall. "The legendary Heavenly Dungeon. Looks like a ruin."

"Looks are deceiving, Chen Wei," the woman said. Her voice was calm, measured, and completely devoid of emotion. "The Heavenly Court did not fall because it was weak. It fell because it was betrayed."

Wei Chen's breath caught. Betrayed? The core had never mentioned betrayal. It had only shown him the final battle, the darkness consuming the court, the Jade Emperor's last stand. But not why.

"In any case," the woman continued, "our mission is clear. Assess the dungeon's current state. Determine if it has chosen a new master. And if it has..."

She let the sentence hang.

"Kill them?" the young man asked eagerly.

"Observe," she corrected. "The Heavenly Court's secrets are worth more than a single life. The right to bind oneself to this dungeon is worth more than a thousand."

Wei Chen's hands trembled. He forced them still. These weren't cultivators coming to test themselves against the dungeon's trials. They were spies. Scouts. Sent by some sect to claim the Heavenly Court's power for themselves.

The core pulsed. *They will not find what they seek.*

"How can you be sure?" Wei Chen whispered.

*Because they seek a master. They do not seek a servant. The Heavenly Dungeon does not bind to those who wish to dominate it.*

"That's... poetic. And terrifying."

*I have had ten thousand years to think on such things.*

The woman walked slowly through the hall, her fingers trailing along the golden pillars. She stopped at Guardian One, studying its blank face with narrowed eyes. "A construct. Crude but functional. The new master is not skilled."

"Should we find them?" the weathered man asked.

"No. They will come to us. Or they will reveal themselves through their dungeon's defenses." She turned in a slow circle, taking in the entire hall. "This floor is incomplete. Only the barest foundations of a trial. The dungeon is still growing, which means the master is still learning."

She smiled. It was not a pleasant expression.

"We have time."

Wei Chen's mind raced. He had to do something. Let them explore, and they'd find the meditation room, the incomplete formations, the core itself. And if they found the core...

He didn't want to think about what would happen.

"Core," he whispered, "can I activate the illusions now?"

*They are not yet in position. The formations are designed to trigger in sequence. If you activate them prematurely, they will be less effective.*

"I don't need effective. I need distracting."

A pause. Then: *Understood. Activating first layer.*

The hall changed.

Golden light that had pulsed along the walls flickered once, twice, then died completely. Shadows rushed in to fill the void, thick and absolute. The woman stopped mid-stride, her hand going to the hilt of her sword.

"What is this?" the young man asked, his arrogance cracking.

"Stay calm," the woman said. "It's an illusion. The dungeon is testing us."

But her voice had lost some of its certainty.

The shadows deepened. Shapes moved in the darkness—vague, formless things that might have been people, might have been beasts, might have been nothing at all. The air grew cold, then cold, then cold enough that the young man's breath began to fog.

"Senior Sister," he said, "I think—"

"Silence."

The woman closed her eyes. When she opened them, they glowed with purple light. "I see through your tricks, dungeon. Show yourself."

The shadows writhed. And then, from the center of the hall, a voice spoke.

It was not Wei Chen's voice. It was something older, deeper, resonant with the weight of ten thousand years. The core was speaking through him, using his qi as a conduit.

"Who comes before the Heavenly Court?"

The woman's eyes widened. For a moment—just a moment—she looked genuinely afraid. Then she composed herself, bowing deeply.

"I am Elder Ling Yue of the Violet Cloud Sect. I come seeking knowledge of the Heavenly Court's fall. My sect wishes to pay our respects to whatever remains of the Jade Emperor's legacy."

The core's voice rumbled through the hall. "The Jade Emperor's legacy is not for those who come with hidden blades."

Elder Ling Yue's face froze. Her hand, Wei Chen noticed, had drifted to the jade hairpin in her hair—a weapon disguised as ornament.

"I do not know what you mean," she said carefully.

"Your hairpin contains a killing formation. Your robes are lined with defensive arrays. Your companions carry weapons designed to sever spiritual bonds." The core's voice grew colder. "Did you think the Heavenly Court would not recognize the tools of assassins?"

The hall exploded into motion.

Elder Ling Yue's hairpin flew from her hair, transforming mid-air into a sword of crystallized lightning. The young man drew his blade, spiritual energy flaring around him like a wildfire. The weathered man simply vanished, reappearing at the hall's eastern wall, hands glowing with sealing techniques.

"We take the core," Elder Ling Yue commanded. "Kill anything that moves."

Wei Chen's heart hammered against his ribs. This was it. This was the moment he died—killed by cultivators before he'd even finished building his first real floor.

But the core was not done.

The shadows surged.

From every corner of the hall, darkness erupted like water from a broken dam. It wasn't just illusion anymore—the core was spending qi, burning through its reserves to create physical constructs. Shadows took the shape of armored warriors, their eyes burning with golden light.

"Fall back!" Elder Ling Yue shouted, but the young man was already engaged, his blade cutting through the first shadow warrior like it was made of smoke.

It was.

The blade passed through without resistance, and the warrior reformed, its golden eyes mocking.

"These are just illusions!" the young man cried. "They can't hurt us!"

"Don't be a fool!" the weathered man yelled. "Illusions that can't hurt you are meant to distract you!"

He was right. While the young man had been swinging at shadows, Wei Chen had been moving. He slipped through the darkness, using the formations the core had taught him, navigating the hall like a fish through water.

He reached the core's chamber just as Elder Ling Yue's lightning sword carved through the door.

She stood in the doorway, her purple robes billowing with unleashed power. Her eyes found him instantly, and her smile returned—sharp, predatory.

"Ah. There you are."

Wei Chen's back hit the wall. The core pulsed at his feet, its golden light flickering weakly. He'd used too much of its energy on the illusions. There was nothing left for defense.

"I don't want to fight you," he said, his voice steadier than he felt.

"Good. Then don't." Elder Ling Yue raised her lightning sword. "Just tell me how to bind the core. I might let you live."

Wei Chen looked down at the core. It pulsed once, weakly, and he felt its presence brush against his mind—not a command, not a plea, but something softer.

*Thank you.*

For what? For trying? For failing?

"No," he said, and he meant it with every fiber of his being. "I won't."

Elder Ling Yue's sword began to descend.

And then the world went white.

The light was absolute, blinding, pure. It came from everywhere and nowhere, burning through the darkness, through the shadows, through the illusions. Elder Ling Yue screamed, dropping her sword, clutching at her eyes.

Wei Chen felt something grab his shoulder—not a hand, but a presence. Warm. Ancient. Familiar.

A voice spoke in his mind, and it was not the core's.

*Well done, little tomb raider. You have passed the first test.*

The light faded.

Elder Ling Yue was gone. The weathered man was gone. The young man was gone. The hall was empty, silent, untouched by the battle that had raged moments before.

And standing in the center of the audience hall, where Guardian One had stood, was a figure of solid gold.

It was a warrior, tall and proud, its armor etched with characters so ancient Wei Chen couldn't read them. Its face was no longer blank. It had features now—a stern jaw, a straight nose, eyes that burned with the light of a thousand suns.

It turned to face Wei Chen, and when it spoke, its voice was the voice of the core, but stronger, clearer, more alive.

"I am Zhenwu. The Perfected Warrior. The Dark Emperor of the North." It bowed, one knee touching the jade floor. "I have been summoned to serve the new Jade Emperor."

Wei Chen stared.

"What."

Zhenwu rose, its golden eyes meeting his. "The first trial is complete. You have defended the core against invaders. You have refused to surrender. You have proven worthy of the Heavenly Court's protection." A pause. "I am that protection."

"But—how—I didn't—"

"You did more than you

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Demon's Bargain

The cultivator's name was Zhao Ming, and he was dying.

Wei Chen knew this the moment the man stumbled through the entrance of the Celestial Audience Hall. His robes—once fine silk embroidered with the emblem of the Azure Cloud Sect—were torn and bloodied. His face was pale, lips blue, and a dark wound festered on his left side, tendrils of black qi crawling outward like living veins.

"Help," Zhao Ming gasped, collapsing onto the jade floor. "Please... the demon... it's coming."

Wei Chen's heart seized. He'd been expecting cultivators tomorrow. He'd prepared formations, illusions, a guardian that could barely hold a spear. He had not prepared for a dying man to stumble through his dungeon entrance before the first floor was even finished.

"Stay calm," Wei Chen said, rushing to the cultivator's side. He knelt, examining the wound. The black qi was familiar—he'd seen it before in tombs sealed with dark curses. This was no ordinary injury. This was a soul-rot, a corruption that ate at the very foundation of a cultivator's spirit.

"Where did this come from?" Wei Chen demanded.

Zhao Ming coughed, blood flecking his lips. "The second floor."

Wei Chen froze. "There is no second floor."

"There is now." Zhao Ming's eyes, wild with fear, fixed on Wei Chen. "The dungeon... it's growing. Faster than you know. While you were building your little meditation room and your pathetic guardian, something else was building too. Something dark."

The core pulsed within Wei Chen's chest, a surge of alarm that made him gasp.

*He speaks truth. I felt it, but could not understand. A corruption has taken root in the depths.*

"How?" Wei Chen hissed, both to the man and to the core. "I'm the dungeon master. I control everything."

Zhao Ming laughed, a wet, broken sound. "You control nothing. The Heavenly Court fell for a reason, boy. The old gods didn't just die—they were *consumed*. And what consumed them... it's still here. Buried in the foundations. Waiting for new flesh to wear."

The words hit Wei Chen like a physical blow. He remembered the darkness he'd felt when he first entered the dungeon, the lingering presence of something vast and hungry. He'd thought it was just the residual energy of the fallen court. He'd been wrong.

"What is it?" Wei Chen asked, his voice low.

"The Demon of the Broken Throne," Zhao Ming whispered. "That's what we called it in the Azure Cloud Sect. Legend says it was once a celestial general, second only to the Jade Emperor himself. When the Heavenly Court fell, he tried to claim the throne for his own. The core rejected him, shattered his spirit, scattered his essence through the dungeon's deepest floors. But he didn't die. He *waited*."

Wei Chen's hands trembled. He looked at the golden pillars, the beautiful murals, the illusionary formations he'd so painstakingly created. It all felt like a child's drawing now, a fragile castle built on a volcano.

"How do I stop him?"

"You don't." Zhao Ming's eyes were starting to glaze over. "He's already won. The second floor is his domain now. He's been feeding on the qi of every cultivator who ventured too deep, growing stronger, rebuilding his form. By now, he's strong enough to manifest. Strong enough to challenge you for control of the core."

"Then why did you come here?" Wei Chen grabbed the man's shoulders, shaking him. "Why warn me?"

"Because I was a fool." Zhao Ming smiled, a sad, resigned expression. "I thought I could steal from the dungeon. Claim a treasure, sell it, buy my way into a better sect. Instead, I found him. He let me go. Told me to deliver a message."

Wei Chen's blood ran cold. "What message?"

Zhao Ming's voice faded to a whisper, his eyes closing. "He said... the new Jade Emperor... will kneel before the old general. Or he will die like the rest."

The cultivator went still. The black qi in his wound pulsed once, twice, then spread across his entire body, turning his skin to ash. Within seconds, Zhao Ming's corpse crumbled into a pile of gray dust, leaving nothing behind but his torn robes and a single jade token.

Wei Chen stared at the remains, his mind racing. The core pulsed again, this time with something that felt almost like fear.

*We are not ready.*

"No shit," Wei Chen muttered. He picked up the jade token, turning it over in his hands. It was warm, inscribed with characters he couldn't read. A key, perhaps. Or a trap.

He pocketed it anyway.

For the next hour, Wei Chen paced the Audience Hall, thinking. The Guardian stood motionless in the corner, its blank face staring at nothing. The illusionary formations hummed softly, waiting for intruders who would never come. The meditation room sat empty, its cushion pristine.

Everything he'd built was meaningless if the second floor was already lost.

"There has to be a way," he said aloud. "You were the Heavenly Court. You ruled the universe for ten thousand years. Surely you have some trick, some failsafe."

The core was silent.

"What about the memories? The archives you showed me before. Is there anything about fighting demons? About reclaiming corrupted floors?"

*A floor, once corrupted, cannot be reclaimed. It must be destroyed and rebuilt.*

"Then destroy it."

*I cannot. The demon has rooted himself too deep. Destroying the second floor would collapse the first, and likely kill you in the process.*

Wei Chen stopped pacing. He turned to face the core's golden glow, which pulsed weakly from the center of the wall. "Then what do I do? Surrender? Let this 'Broken Throne' take over, turn the dungeon into his personal fortress, and then spread his corruption across the world?"

*There is one option. But it is... dangerous.*

"I'm a tomb raider who accidentally became the Jade Emperor's replacement. Everything about my life is dangerous. Spit it out."

The core pulsed again, and a new set of images flooded Wei Chen's mind. He saw a dark room, deep beneath the dungeon, where chains of pure light bound a figure made of shadow. The figure was huge, monstrous, with too many limbs and too many eyes. But at its center, there was a faint glow—a fragment of something pure.

*The demon's essence was shattered when he was cast out. This fragment is what remains of his original celestial nature. It is buried deep, forgotten, locked away by the old gods before they fell.*

"Let me guess," Wei Chen said slowly. "You want me to go down there, find this fragment, and use it to... what? Purify him? Turn him back into a celestial general?"

*No. The general is gone. Only the demon remains. But the fragment can be used to create a new guardian. A counterweight. A being strong enough to challenge the demon and reclaim the second floor.*

Wei Chen considered this. It was insane. The second floor was already corrupted, and the demon was strong enough to manifest a physical form. Going down there—even to the deepest depths where the fragment was hidden—was suicide.

But staying here meant certain death anyway. The demon would come for him eventually. And when he did, Wei Chen's illusions and his pathetic Guardian One would be nothing more than a brief snack.

"Fine," he said, his voice steady despite the terror clawing at his chest. "Tell me how to get there."

The core showed him a map. A hidden passage, located behind the throne in the Audience Hall. A staircase that spiraled down into darkness, bypassing the corrupted second floor entirely. It would take him straight to the prison chamber where the fragment was held.

"There's just one problem," Wei Chen said, studying the map. "If I go down there, I'm leaving the first floor completely unguarded. The demon could just waltz in and take you while I'm gone."

*He cannot take me. I am bound to you. If I am destroyed, you die. If you die, I am freed to choose another master. But the demon cannot claim me without killing you first.*

"That's... surprisingly reassuring. And also terrifying." Wei Chen took a deep breath. "Alright. No time like the present."

He walked to the throne—a massive, ornate seat of jade and gold that dominated the far end of the Audience Hall. Behind it, the wall was smooth, unbroken. But when he pressed his hand against it and channeled qi into the surface, the stone rippled like water, revealing a narrow doorway.

Darkness stared back at him. Cold, absolute, hungry darkness.

Wei Chen lit a small flame with his qi, holding it aloft as he stepped through. The doorway sealed behind him, plunging him into a world of shadows and silence.

The staircase was old, older than anything he'd seen in the dungeon so far. The steps were worn smooth by countless feet, and the walls were covered in carvings—battles between celestials and demons, gods falling from the sky, a throne cracking in half. The history of the Heavenly Court's fall, etched into stone.

He descended for what felt like hours. The air grew colder, heavier, and the light from his qi flame flickered as if something was breathing on it.

Then he heard it.

A whisper. Soft at first, then louder, coming from all directions at once.

*Come closer, little master. I've been waiting.*

Wei Chen stopped, his heart pounding. "Who's there?"

*You know who I am. You've already heard my message. But I wanted to meet you face to face. Or... as close to a face as I can manage.*

The shadows ahead of him twisted, coalescing into a form. It was humanoid, but wrong—too tall, too thin, with arms that bent at impossible angles. Its face was a mask of shifting darkness, eyes like twin pits of fire.

The Demon of the Broken Throne.

"You're supposed to be on the second floor," Wei Chen said, trying to keep his voice steady.

*I am everywhere, little master. The corruption spreads. It reaches. It grows. Did you really think a hidden passage would keep me out?*

Wei Chen's hand went to his pocket, where the jade token sat. A key? A trap? He'd find out soon enough.

"I'm not here to fight you," he said. "I'm here to take back what's mine."

The demon laughed, a sound like grinding bones. *You? Take back? You are a grave robber. A thief. A nothing. The core chose you by accident, and it will discard you just as easily.*

"Maybe." Wei Chen pulled out the jade token, holding it up. "But accidents have a way of becoming destiny."

The demon's eyes widened—or at least, the pits of fire seemed to flare. *Where did you get that?*

"From a dead man. He said you let him go. That was your first mistake."

Wei Chen didn't wait for a response. He threw the token into the darkness, and as it fell, it began to glow. The light spread, pushing back the shadows, and the demon screamed—a sound of pure, undiluted rage.

The token was a key. Not to a door. To a *seal*.

And Wei Chen had just unlocked it.

The floor beneath him cracked, and a chasm opened, swallowing the demon, the darkness, and Wei Chen himself.

He fell.

And as he fell, he heard the core's voice, distant

title: The First Trial Begins
content:
The morning of the fourth day dawned gray and cold. Wei Chen stood at the entrance of his dungeon—the grand gates of what had once been the Southern Heavenly Gate—and watched the mist curl around the broken pillars. His stomach was a knot of anxiety and excitement.

They were coming.

He'd spent the night double-checking every formation, every trap, every illusion. The first floor was as ready as it would ever be. Guardian One stood motionless in the audience hall, spear gleaming. The meditation room hummed with refined qi. And the illusion arrays were woven so tightly into the fabric of the hall that even Wei Chen had trouble seeing through them.

But would it be enough?

He didn't know. The cultivators coming today were from the Azure Cloud Sect, one of the minor sects that had sprung up in the chaos after the Heavenly Court's fall. They weren't powerful by ancient standards, but they were still cultivators. Men and women who had spent years training their bodies and sharpening their qi.

Wei Chen was a tomb raider who had stumbled into godhood by accident.

"Focus," he muttered to himself. "You're the dungeon master now. Act like it."

The core pulsed in agreement. *They approach.*

Wei Chen straightened his robes—plain gray cloth, nothing fancy—and walked through the gates to meet them.

There were five of them. They walked single file along the mountain path, their footsteps sure and steady despite the treacherous terrain. Leading them was a young woman in azure robes, her hair tied in a high ponytail, a sword at her hip. Her eyes were sharp, scanning the ruined buildings with barely concealed hunger.

Behind her came four others—two men, two women, all wearing the same azure robes, all carrying weapons. Swords, mostly, but one of the men carried a heavy staff, and one of the women had a guqin slung across her back. A music cultivator. Interesting.

The leader stopped when she saw Wei Chen. Her eyes narrowed.

"You are the dungeon master?"

Wei Chen bowed, keeping his movements smooth and practiced. "I am. Welcome to the Heavenly Dungeon. I trust your journey was pleasant?"

"Pleasant?" The leader's lips curled. "We climbed a mountain that shouldn't exist, passed through forests that changed direction when we weren't looking, and nearly fell into a ravine that appeared out of nowhere. Pleasant is not the word I would use."

Wei Chen kept his face neutral. He hadn't set those obstacles. The mountain itself was responding to the dungeon's awakening, reshaping the terrain to protect its master. That was... unexpected. And useful.

"Apologies," he said. "The dungeon is young. Its influence extends further than I anticipated."

The leader studied him for a long moment. Then she nodded, apparently satisfied. "I am Senior Disciple Lin Mei of the Azure Cloud Sect. These are my companions. We have come to challenge your dungeon, as per the notice you sent to the sects."

The notice. Right. Wei Chen had almost forgotten. On the second day, the core had instructed him to send out a spiritual broadcast—a call to cultivators, inviting them to test the new dungeon. It was how dungeons attracted adventurers, the core had explained. Without challengers, a dungeon would starve.

"Then you are welcome," Wei Chen said, stepping aside. "The first floor awaits."

Lin Mei walked past him without another word. Her companions followed, their eyes wary, their hands never straying far from their weapons.

Wei Chen watched them enter the gates, his heart pounding.

The first trial had begun.

---

Lin Mei stopped at the entrance to the audience hall.

The room was vast, larger than it had any right to be given the size of the building outside. Golden pillars stretched toward a ceiling lost in mist. The jade floor gleamed underfoot, reflecting the light of a thousand lamps that burned with no visible source.

At the center of the hall stood Guardian One.

The construct had changed since Wei Chen first created it. Its armor was more detailed now, the golden light that formed its body pulsing with a slow, steady rhythm. Its spear was no longer a simple shaft of light—it had developed a blade, sharp and gleaming, etched with characters that glowed faintly.

It looked dangerous.

Lin Mei's hand went to her sword. "A celestial soldier," she breathed. "I've only read about them in ancient texts."

"It's a construct," one of her companions said—the man with the staff. "A spiritual puppet. It has no soul."

"Soul or not, it's blocking our path." Lin Mei drew her sword. The blade sang as it left the sheath, a clear, pure note. "Form up. We advance together."

The five cultivators spread out, weapons ready. The music cultivator pulled her guqin from her back, her fingers already dancing across the strings. A low hum filled the air, and Wei Chen felt the qi in the room shift, responding to her melody.

Guardian One raised its spear.

The battle was over in seconds.

Lin Mei moved first, her sword cutting a silver arc through the air. Guardian One met the strike with its spear, the clash of weapons sending sparks cascading across the floor. The staff-wielder circled around, aiming for the construct's flank, while the other two swordsmen pressed from the front.

The music cultivator's song changed, becoming sharper, more urgent. The sound waves lashed at Guardian One like invisible whips, tearing at its golden form.

Wei Chen watched from the shadows, his fists clenched. Guardian One was holding its own, but barely. For every strike it landed, it took three in return. Its golden light was flickering, dimming.

*It's not enough,* he thought. *They're going to break through.*

But then something unexpected happened.

As Guardian One took a particularly heavy blow from Lin Mei's sword, its featureless face turned. Toward Wei Chen. Toward the core.

And it *changed.*

The golden light flared, blindingly bright. When it faded, Guardian One was different. Its armor had grown more ornate, more detailed. A helmet now covered its head, and from beneath that helmet, two eyes glowed—not gold, but *silver.*

It spoke.

"Intruders detected. Authorization: none. Initiating full combat protocol."

Lin Mei's eyes went wide. "It can *talk?*"

Guardian One didn't answer. It moved.

The spear became a blur of light and death. The staff-wielder went down first, the flat of the blade catching him across the temple. He crumpled like a puppet with its strings cut. One of the swordsmen followed a moment later, his weapon shattered, his arm broken.

Lin Mei retreated, her face pale. "What is this? Dungeons aren't supposed to have talking guardians!"

She was right. Wei Chen knew that. Dungeons created beasts, traps, obstacles—not intelligent soldiers that could adapt and learn. This was something new. Something the core had never shown him.

*What are you?* he asked the core silently.

The core pulsed. *I am the heart of the Heavenly Court. I am the memory of ten thousand years of celestial rule. I am more than a dungeon.*

*You created a guardian with intelligence.*

*I gave it what it needed to protect itself. To protect you.*

Guardian One advanced on Lin Mei, spear raised for the killing blow. The music cultivator was screaming, her fingers tearing at the guqin strings, but her sound attacks did nothing. The construct was stronger now, its golden light repelling the spiritual assault like water off a stone.

"Stop," Wei Chen said.

The word came out before he could think. Guardian One froze, its spear inches from Lin Mei's throat.

The cultivator's eyes were wide, her breath coming in ragged gasps. She stared at Wei Chen, and for the first time, he saw fear in her gaze.

"You're the dungeon master," she whispered. "You control this thing."

"I do."

"Then call it off. We surrender. We failed the trial."

Wei Chen looked at her. At the unconscious cultivators on the floor. At the music cultivator, who had stopped playing and was now clutching her guqin like a shield.

He could kill them. The dungeon had given him that power. One command, and Guardian One would finish what it started. Their qi would be absorbed into the core, making the dungeon stronger.

But that wasn't what a dungeon was supposed to do.

*Trials,* the core had said. *Challenges. Tests of strength and spirit. Not murder.*

"Let them go," Wei Chen said.

Guardian One lowered its spear. Lin Mei scrambled backward, helping her companions to their feet. The music cultivator followed, her face pale, her hands shaking.

At the entrance, Lin Mei turned back. "This isn't over," she said. "The Azure Cloud Sect will not forget this humiliation. We will return with stronger disciples. With elders."

"I look forward to it," Wei Chen said.

He didn't feel as confident as he sounded.

---

After the cultivators left, Wei Chen sat in the meditation room, staring at the formations on the floor.

Guardian One stood outside, its silver eyes scanning the empty hall. It had not returned to its original state. It remained changed, upgraded, more powerful. And it still had that spark of intelligence that Wei Chen hadn't put there.

*You need to explain,* he said to the core.

*The dungeon evolves. I evolve. You evolve. This is natural.*

*Natural? You created a sentient guardian without telling me!*

*I created what was necessary. The Azure Cloud Sect will return. Others will come. You needed a general, not a soldier.*

Wei Chen rubbed his temples. "What else aren't you telling me?"

The core was silent.

"Answer me."

*There are things you are not ready to know.*

"I'm the dungeon master! I should know everything!"

*The previous Jade Emperor knew everything. It destroyed him. Some truths are poison. Some knowledge is a curse.*

Wei Chen opened his mouth to argue, but a tremor ran through the dungeon. Not physical—spiritual. A disturbance in the fabric of the dungeon's existence.

"What was that?"

The core pulsed, and for the first time, Wei Chen felt something he hadn't felt before from the ancient artifact.

*Fear.*

*Something approaches. Something ancient. Something that should not exist.*

"What is it?"

The core showed him an image. A crack in the sky above the dungeon. Not a physical tear, but a wound in reality itself. Through that crack, something was reaching. A hand. Massive, skeletal, wrapped in shadows that moved like living things.

*The Abyss,* the core whispered. *It has found us.*

The hand grasped the edges of the tear, and the sky shattered.

Wei Chen stood frozen as a figure began to emerge from the void—a figure that radiated death, decay, and the cold emptiness of a thousand forgotten graves.

It turned its hollow eyes toward the dungeon.

And it smiled.

title: The First Trial Begins
content:
The morning of the fourth day arrived with a weight that pressed down on Wei Chen’s shoulders like a physical force. He stood in the Celestial Audience Hall, watching the golden light pulse along the walls, and tried to convince himself he was ready.

He wasn’t.

The illusion formations were in place. Guardian One stood at attention in the corner, its blank face somehow managing to look determined. The meditation room hummed with refined qi, and the main hall itself had been transformed into a maze of spiritual traps and hidden passages. It wasn’t much. It was barely anything. But it was all he had.

“They’ll come at noon,” he muttered, checking his formation notes for the hundredth time. “Three Qi Condensation cultivators. Maybe four.”

The core pulsed. *You are afraid.*

“Terrified,” Wei Chen admitted. “But that doesn’t change what needs to be done.”

*Good. Fear without paralysis is wisdom.*

He snorted. “Did you learn that from the Jade Emperor himself?”

*No. I learned it from watching him fall.*

The words hit harder than they should have. Wei Chen fell silent, staring at the core’s glow. Ten thousand years ago, the most powerful being in existence had stood where he was standing now. And he had fallen. What chance did a tomb raider have?

The ground trembled.

Wei Chen’s head snapped up. “They’re early.”

He felt them before he saw them—three presences, sharp and aggressive, pushing through the dungeon’s outer barrier like knives through silk. They weren’t subtle. They weren’t respectful. They were exactly the kind of cultivators he’d been warned about.

“Showtime,” he whispered.

He touched the core, and the audience hall shifted around him. The golden light dimmed to a soft amber, the mist thickened, and the formations hummed to life. From the outside, the hall would appear empty. Abandoned. A ruin waiting to be looted.

That was the bait.

The first cultivator stepped through the entrance.

He was a big man, broad-shouldered and scarred, with a cultivation base that radiated brute force rather than refined skill. Qi Condensation third layer, Wei Chen guessed—strong enough to be dangerous, weak enough to be arrogant. Behind him came two more: a woman in flowing white robes, her face sharp and calculating, and a thin man with quick eyes and quicker hands.

The big man laughed. “Would you look at this place? The old man wasn’t lying. There really is a dungeon here.”

The woman’s eyes swept the hall. “It’s smaller than I expected. And emptier.”

“Empty means easy pickings,” the thin man said. His fingers twitched. “I can feel the qi. It’s weak, but it’s here. The core must be nearby.”

Wei Chen watched from the shadows, his heart pounding. The illusions were working. They couldn’t see him. They couldn’t see the formations. They saw only what he wanted them to see.

*First step complete,* the core whispered. *Now show them why the Heavenly Court does not suffer thieves.*

Wei Chen activated the first formation.

The floor beneath the big man’s feet dissolved into golden mist. He dropped like a stone, crashing through the illusion into the pit below—a pit that Wei Chen had spent half the night digging, lined with broken jade and sharp-edged stones. The big man landed hard, his leg twisting at an unnatural angle. He screamed.

“Ambush!” the woman shouted, her robes flaring as she leaped backward. Her hand went to her sword. “Formation master! There’s a formation master here!”

The thin man was already moving, his hands tracing patterns in the air. “I’ll break it. Give me time.”

Wei Chen’s blood ran cold. A formation breaker. Of course. Of course they’d brought one.

He didn’t wait. He activated the second formation, and the walls began to close in.

The woman’s eyes widened. She drew her sword and slashed at the golden light, her qi flaring bright enough to hurt Wei Chen’s eyes. The blade cut through the illusion like paper, but the real wall was right behind it, and her strike left a dent in the jade instead of shattering it.

“It’s layered!” she snarled. “The illusion is layered over the real structure. We can’t just cut our way through.”

The thin man was sweating now, his hands moving faster. “I see it. I almost have it. Just—hold them off.”

Wei Chen didn’t give him the chance.

He sent Guardian One forward.

The construct moved with surprising speed, its spear aimed at the thin man’s chest. The formation breaker yelped and threw himself sideways, his concentration shattering. The formations flickered.

“No!” Wei Chen hissed. He poured more qi into the core, trying to stabilize them, but the damage was done. The woman saw the weakness. She drove her sword into the flickering point, and the entire illusion collapsed like a house of cards.

The audience hall returned to normal.

Wei Chen stood revealed, one hand on the core, his face pale.

The woman’s eyes locked onto him. “You.”

“Me,” he said weakly.

She smiled. It was not a friendly smile. “I was wondering what kind of fool would claim a dungeon for himself. A Qi Condensation first layer? Barely? You’re not even a proper cultivator.”

“I’m learning.”

“You’ll die learning.”

She moved like water, her sword a blur of silver light. Wei Chen threw himself backward, but he was too slow. The blade caught him across the chest, tearing through his robes and leaving a shallow but bloody gash. He hit the ground hard, pain exploding through his ribs.

*Get up,* the core urged. *Get up now.*

He scrambled to his feet, blood soaking his shirt. The woman advanced, her sword dripping. Behind her, the thin man was recovering, his hands already tracing new patterns. The big man was still in the pit, cursing and trying to climb out.

Three against one. And he was bleeding.

“You should have run when you had the chance,” the woman said. “This dungeon was never meant for the likes of you.”

Wei Chen wiped blood from his mouth. “Maybe not. But it’s mine now.”

He slammed his hand against the core.

The core blazed.

Golden light erupted from the floor, the walls, the ceiling—every surface of the dungeon suddenly alive with power. The woman staggered, shielding her eyes. The thin man screamed as the light burned through his defensive qi. Even the big man fell silent.

And then the light faded.

When Wei Chen could see again, the cultivators were gone.

No—not gone. They were still there. But the dungeon had changed.

They stood in a vast hall, far larger than the Celestial Audience Hall had been. Pillars of jade and gold stretched up into an impossibly high ceiling, where clouds swirled and thunder rumbled. The floor was made of polished starstone, reflecting the light of a thousand floating lanterns. And at the far end of the hall, on a throne that seemed to be carved from pure light itself, sat a figure.

The figure was Wei Chen.

But not the Wei Chen of now. This was a Wei Chen transformed—robed in celestial silk, crowned with a circlet of stars, his eyes burning with the authority of a god. He looked down at the three cultivators with an expression of cold amusement.

“Welcome,” the throne-Wei Chen said, his voice echoing through the hall, “to the first trial of the Heavenly Dungeon.”

The real Wei Chen stared, his mouth hanging open. *What—*

*The memory technique,* the core said, its voice strained but triumphant. *I adapted it. Made it real. For a few minutes, this hall will be what it once was. And you will be what you must become.*

“I don’t—I can’t—”

*You can. You are the core’s master. Act like it.*

Wei Chen took a breath. He looked at the cultivators—the woman, her sword trembling; the thin man, his confidence shattered; the big man, still in his pit, staring up at the throne with naked fear.

He didn’t know what he was doing. He didn’t know if this would work.

But he knew one thing: he would not let the Heavenly Court fall again.

“You came here seeking treasure,” the throne-Wei Chen said, his voice resonating with ancient power. “You found something far greater. You found a trial.”

The woman’s jaw tightened. “What kind of trial?”

“The kind that kills you if you fail.”

The hall rumbled. The lanterns flickered. And the throne-Wei Chen raised his hand.

“First trial: face the guardian of the eastern gate.”

From the shadows at the edge of the hall, something stepped forward.

It was massive—twice the height of a man, built of golden light and jade armor, its face a roaring lion’s mask. In its hands, it held a spear that crackled with lightning.

The cultivators paled.

The real Wei Chen felt the core shudder. *This will drain everything I have. The illusion will last only as long as I can sustain it. Use the time wisely.*

“How long?” he whispered.

*Three minutes. Maybe four.*

Wei Chen looked at the guardian, at the cultivators, at the throne he was somehow both sitting on and watching from afar.

Three minutes.

He could work with that.

The guardian roared, and the battle began.

title: The Third Floor's Awakening
content:
The morning of the fourth day dawned gray and cold. Wei Chen stood at the entrance of the Heavenly Dungeon, watching the mist curl around the broken pillars of the old celestial palace. His body ached from three days of relentless work, but his mind was sharp, alive with the possibilities the dungeon had shown him.

Guardian One stood behind him, still featureless, still silent. Wei Chen had spent the night reinforcing its spiritual core, feeding it fragments of his own qi until he felt lightheaded. The construct could now move with some semblance of grace, its spear strikes carrying enough force to stagger a Qi Condensation cultivator.

It wasn't enough. It would never be enough.

*They come,* the core pulsed.

Wei Chen tensed. "How many?"

*Three. Two Qi Condensation. One Foundation Establishment.*

"Foundation Establishment?" Wei Chen's blood ran cold. "That's—that's supposed to be impossible. The dungeon's suppression array should block anyone above Qi Condensation from entering."

*The array is damaged. Has been for centuries. The Foundation Establishment cultivator will feel weakened, but not blocked.*

"Great. Just great." Wei Chen ran his hands through his hair, pacing. "One Foundation Establishment could wipe out everything I've built in minutes."

*Not if you use the illusions.*

"The illusions are untested. I've only had them for a day."

*Then test them now.*

Wei Chen took a deep breath. The core was right. He had no other choice. He turned and walked back into the audience hall, the golden light pulsing around him like a heartbeat. He positioned Guardian One behind a pillar, out of sight, and retreated to the meditation room.

The entrance shimmered as he activated the first illusion array. To anyone looking, the audience hall would now appear empty—no golden light, no murals, just a vast, dark chamber filled with dust and shadows. The floor would seem cracked, the pillars broken. It looked like a ruin.

That was the first layer. The second layer would activate when the cultivators reached the center of the hall. They would see shadows moving in the corners, hear whispers that weren't there. If they panicked, the illusions would grow stronger, feeding on their fear.

Wei Chen settled into the meditation room, closing his eyes. He could feel the dungeon like an extension of his own body—every stone, every breath of air, every flicker of light. The cultivators were close now. He could sense their qi approaching, two bright flames and one blazing bonfire.

They entered.
